persons.decision_style: Senatorial, consensus-building; handed off to Rubin mid-term.
persons.track_record: 1993 deficit-reduction package; early Mexican peso strain; NAFTA-era trade finance.
persons.vulnerabilities: Short tenure; overshadowed by his successor's longer imprint.
persons.relationships: Predecessor and mentor-figure to Rubin's Treasury.
persons.outlook: Historical opener of the 1990s fiscal-consolidation chapter.

persons.name: Robert Rubin
persons.bluf: The defining revolving-door archetype: Goldman Sachs co-chair → Treasury → Citigroup. Assess with HIGH confidence he co-led the bloc (with Greenspan and Summers) that crushed Brooksley Born's OTC-derivatives review and drove Glass-Steagall repeal — decisions widely tied to the 2008 architecture. THE on-thesis figure for regulatory capture.
persons.drivers: Wall-Street worldview; faith in market self-regulation; 'strong dollar' doctrine; deep-bench network influence ('the Rubin tree').
persons.worldview: Probabilistic, market-efficient (his 'In an Uncertain World' framing); believed deregulated finance optimizes capital allocation.
persons.decision_style: Quiet, consensus-forging, intellectually self-assured; preferred to work the room over public confrontation.
persons.track_record: Strong-dollar policy; Mexican and Asian crisis bailouts; Gramm-Leach-Bliley (Glass-Steagall repeal, 1999); blocked CFTC derivatives oversight; then $115M+ at Citigroup as it built the exposures that nearly killed it.
persons.vulnerabilities: The Born episode and Citigroup tenure are the central indictments of the capture thesis; called Enron contacts and Citi's bailout into question.
persons.relationships: Allied with Greenspan/Summers against Born; protégés (Geithner, Lew, Froman) populated later administrations.
persons.outlook: The keystone node connecting deregulation, the revolving door, and the moral-hazard lineage.
persons.confidence: HIGH on the deregulation record (documented); interpretation on systemic causation labeled.

persons.name: Paul O'Neill
persons.bluf: The Alcoa CEO whose blunt dissent got him fired, then turned whistleblower in 'The Price of Loyalty.' Assess with HIGH confidence his account documented an administration that prioritized tax cuts and Iraq over fiscal evidence — a rare insider rupture.
persons.drivers: Engineer-executive's evidence-orientation; institutional candor; discomfort with ideology over analysis.
persons.worldview: Pragmatic, data-driven; skeptical of deficit-financed tax cuts.
persons.decision_style: Outspoken, undiplomatic; collided with the political operation.
persons.track_record: Opposed the second round of tax cuts and questioned Iraq pre-planning; fired Dec 2002; cooperated with Suskind's exposé.
persons.vulnerabilities: Political tin ear; his candor ended his tenure.
persons.relationships: Outsider to the Bush political core; allied intellectually with deficit hawks.
persons.outlook: Historical example of evidence-vs-ideology rupture inside Treasury.

persons.name: John Snow
persons.bluf: The CSX railroad CEO who ran Treasury through the housing-bubble inflation years. Assess with MODERATE confidence his tenure embodied benign neglect of the credit excesses then building toward 2008.
persons.drivers: Corporate-executive pragmatism; loyalty to the growth narrative.
persons.worldview: Pro-growth, deregulatory; saw rising home prices as prosperity, not risk.
persons.decision_style: Low-friction salesman of administration policy; not a risk-watchdog.
persons.track_record: Tax-cut advocacy; weak-dollar drift; presided as subprime origination peaked.
persons.vulnerabilities: Failure to flag the building housing/credit imbalance.
persons.relationships: Succeeded by the far more consequential Paulson as the crisis hit.
persons.outlook: Historical: the quiet pre-crisis Treasury.

persons.name: Henry Paulson
persons.bluf: The Goldman Sachs CEO who became the GFC field general — TARP, the Lehman decision, AIG, Fannie/Freddie conservatorship. Assess with HIGH confidence his backstops stopped the panic while cementing 'too big to fail' as policy reality, deepening the moral-hazard lineage.
persons.drivers: Crisis-manager's pragmatism; institutional-survival imperative; intense work ethic.
persons.worldview: Market-believer forced into massive intervention; 'bazooka' deterrence logic.
persons.decision_style: Decisive, improvisational under fire; the Lehman non-rescue remains the most-debated call of the era.
persons.track_record: $700B TARP; AIG rescue; Fannie/Freddie conservatorship; forced bank capital injections; let Lehman fail (Sept 2008).
persons.vulnerabilities: Goldman conflict-of-interest optics (Goldman benefited from the AIG backstop); the Lehman decision's contested consequences.
persons.relationships: Worked in a triad with Bernanke and Geithner; the Goldman-Treasury pipeline made literal.
persons.outlook: On-thesis anchor of crisis-era backstops and TBTF institutionalization.

persons.name: Timothy Geithner
persons.bluf: The NY Fed president turned Treasury Secretary who designed the post-crisis bank-rescue architecture. Assess with HIGH confidence his stress-test/'foam the runway' approach saved the banks while drawing lasting criticism for favoring creditors over homeowners — a core capture-thesis exhibit.
persons.drivers: Crisis-technocrat's systemic-stability priority; Rubin-protégé worldview; aversion to disorderly failure.
persons.worldview: Pragmatic financial-stability-first; believed restoring bank solvency was the precondition for recovery.
persons.decision_style: Calculated, market-soothing; chose recapitalization and confidence-restoration over punitive measures or aggressive mortgage relief.
persons.track_record: 2009 stress tests (SCAP); TARP execution; HAMP (criticized as inadequate); 'foaming the runway' for banks by stretching out foreclosures (his own phrase).
persons.vulnerabilities: The homeowner-vs-bank tradeoff; later joined Warburg Pincus (revolving door).
persons.relationships: Triad with Bernanke/Paulson; Rubin lineage; continuity to Lew.
persons.outlook: On-thesis figure for the 'banks first' resolution model.

persons.name: Jacob Lew
persons.bluf: The budget technocrat who built modern financial-statecraft — the sanctions architecture that turned the dollar system into a coercive instrument. Assess with HIGH confidence his 'weaponization of finance' doctrine is a direct line from Connally's leverage to today's sanctions regime.
persons.drivers: Process mastery (OMB veteran); belief in finance as a tool of foreign policy; institutional loyalty.
persons.worldview: Pragmatic internationalist; warned (presciently) that over-using sanctions could erode dollar centrality.
persons.decision_style: Detail-driven, methodical; the 'sanctions as smart power' framework was his signature.
persons.track_record: Debt-ceiling-crisis management; Iran sanctions/JCPOA financial terms; his 2016 speech warning against sanctions overreach; later Citi, private equity (Lindsay Goldberg), and Israel ambassador (2024).
persons.vulnerabilities: Citigroup compensation optics; the sanctions tool he refined now cited as accelerating de-dollarization.
persons.relationships: Rubin/Obama lineage; counterpart to sanctioned-state finance officials.
persons.outlook: On-thesis architect of the financial-coercion system whose overuse the project tracks as a de-dollarization driver.

persons.name: Steven Mnuchin
persons.bluf: Goldman → OneWest ('foreclosure machine') → Treasury → Liberty Strategic Capital, a Gulf-backed PE fund tied to AI/data infrastructure. Assess with HIGH confidence he is a live bridge from the revolving-door lineage to the present Gulf-sovereign-AI capital loop the project tracks.
persons.drivers: Deal-maker's opportunism; wealth accumulation; proximity to power; hedge-fund/film-finance background.
persons.worldview: Transactional financier; pro-tax-cut, pro-deregulation, pro-crypto-accommodation later.
persons.decision_style: Pragmatic, media-comfortable; ran the 2017 tax cut and the 2020 CARES/PPP facilities.
persons.track_record: Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (2017); CARES Act corporate facilities; post-Treasury raised Liberty Strategic with Gulf SWF anchors (PIF, Mubadala), investing in cyber/AI/fintech; bid for TikTok.
persons.vulnerabilities: OneWest foreclosure record; conflict optics between Gulf-funded PE and prior official role.
persons.relationships: Goldman/Treasury pipeline; Gulf-SWF ties connect him to the Tahnoon/MBS/MGX nodes in the bubble map.
persons.outlook: On-thesis connector: the regulator-to-Gulf-AI-capital throughline made personal.

persons.name: William Casey
persons.bluf: A rare regulator-turned-spymaster: SEC chair under Nixon, then Reagan's CIA director and central Iran-Contra figure. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his trajectory exemplifies the porous boundary between financial-market oversight and the intelligence apparatus.
persons.drivers: OSS-veteran's covert-operations instinct; conservative ideological commitment; appetite for power.
persons.worldview: Cold-War hawk; saw both markets and intelligence as arenas of strategic contest.
persons.decision_style: Secretive, aggressive, rule-bending (Iran-Contra); mumbling opacity was legendary.
persons.track_record: Early-1970s SEC enforcement; later directed the CIA through the contra war and arms-for-hostages scheme; died as the scandal broke.
persons.vulnerabilities: Iran-Contra culpability; the regulator/spy overlap invites conflict-of-interest scrutiny.
persons.relationships: Reagan inner circle; node linking the SEC and CIA director lineages.
persons.outlook: Historical illustration of regulator-intelligence permeability.

persons.name: John Shad
persons.bluf: The first Wall Streeter (E.F. Hutton vice-chair) to chair the SEC in 50 years, and the architect of Reagan-era market deregulation. Assess with HIGH confidence his deregulate-but-prosecute-insiders posture defined the Boesky/Milken-era enforcement.
persons.drivers: Wall-Street pro-market conviction balanced by a personal disgust for fraud (he endowed an ethics chair).
persons.worldview: Deregulation grows markets; insider trading corrodes trust and must be punished.
persons.decision_style: Pro-deregulation on structure, hard-line on insider trading; the apparent paradox of his tenure.
persons.track_record: Eased securities rules while launching the insider-trading crackdown (Boesky 1986, leading toward Milken); shelf registration (Rule 415).
persons.vulnerabilities: Deregulation enabled the 1980s takeover/junk-bond excesses he simultaneously policed.
persons.relationships: Wall-Street-to-SEC pipeline; precursor to later financier-chairs.
persons.outlook: Historical anchor of the deregulate-and-prosecute model.

persons.name: Arthur Levitt
persons.bluf: The longest-serving SEC chair, an investor-advocate who nonetheless lost the two fights that mattered most — OTC-derivatives oversight and auditor independence. Assess with HIGH confidence those defeats (to the Rubin/Greenspan bloc and the accounting lobby) helped set up Enron and 2008.
persons.drivers: Retail-investor protection ethos (ex-AMEX chair); reputation as 'the investor's advocate.'
persons.worldview: Markets work best with transparency and a level playing field for small investors.
persons.decision_style: Persistent but ultimately outgunned on the biggest structural fights; 'Take on the Street' recounts the losses.
persons.track_record: Plain-English disclosure, Regulation FD (fair disclosure); but his push to bar auditors from consulting for audit clients was beaten back, and he was sidelined on derivatives.
persons.vulnerabilities: Defeated on auditor independence (Enron's enabler) and derivatives (2008's); the limits of a reformer without political backing.
persons.relationships: Antagonist of the accounting lobby and the Rubin/Greenspan/Summers derivatives bloc; ally of Born in spirit if not in outcome.
persons.outlook: On-thesis case study in regulatory capture defeating a well-intentioned chair.

persons.name: Harvey Pitt
persons.bluf: The securities lawyer who took over weeks before 9/11 and Enron, promised a 'kinder, gentler' SEC, and resigned amid the post-Enron firestorm. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his tenure shows how industry-defense credentials backfire in a fraud crisis.
persons.drivers: Technical securities-law expertise (he had represented the accounting firms he now regulated); desire to modernize.
persons.worldview: Cooperative-regulation believer; misjudged the post-Enron public mood.
persons.decision_style: Tone-deaf in crisis (the 'kinder, gentler' line; the Webster PCAOB appointment fiasco) despite real expertise.
persons.track_record: Oversaw Sarbanes-Oxley's passage and the PCAOB's troubled creation; forced out Nov 2002.
persons.vulnerabilities: Prior representation of Big Five auditors created conflict optics during the accounting-fraud wave.
persons.relationships: Industry-defense-bar-to-SEC pipeline; the capture optics that ended him.
persons.outlook: Historical illustration of revolving-door optics in a crisis.

persons.name: William Donaldson
persons.bluf: The DLJ co-founder and former NYSE chair who ran a surprisingly activist post-Enron SEC, splitting from his own party on enforcement. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his hedge-fund-registration push (later struck down) presaged the regulatory gaps 2008 exposed.
persons.drivers: Establishment-reformer's institutional duty; willingness to break with deregulators.
persons.worldview: Markets need credible cops; post-Enron trust must be rebuilt.
persons.decision_style: Independent, coalition-crossing (sided with Democratic commissioners); resigned when the politics turned.
persons.track_record: Sarbanes-Oxley implementation; mutual-fund timing cases; hedge-fund adviser registration (vacated by courts in 2006).
persons.vulnerabilities: His pro-regulation turn alienated the administration that appointed him.
persons.relationships: Wall-Street-founder-turned-regulator; contrast to his successor Cox's lighter touch.
persons.outlook: Historical near-miss: the registration he sought might have surfaced pre-2008 risks.

persons.name: Christopher Cox
persons.bluf: The free-market congressman who chaired the SEC straight into the GFC and the Madoff revelation. Assess with HIGH confidence his light-touch tenure — including the CSE program that let investment banks lever up and the missed Madoff warnings — is a central exhibit in pre-2008 regulatory failure.
persons.drivers: Deregulatory ideology; congressional pro-business record; faith in market discipline.
persons.worldview: Markets self-correct; heavy regulation imposes costs without commensurate benefit.
persons.decision_style: Hands-off until forced (the short-selling ban of Sept 2008 was a panicked reversal); criticized as passive in the crisis.
persons.track_record: Oversaw the Consolidated Supervised Entities program (Bear/Lehman leverage); the SEC ignored repeated Madoff tips; abrupt 2008 short-sale ban.
persons.vulnerabilities: The Madoff oversight failure and CSE collapse define his legacy; 'the SEC was asleep' critique.
persons.relationships: Deregulatory continuity broken by the crisis; succeeded by the rebuilding Schapiro.
persons.outlook: On-thesis anchor of pre-crisis supervisory failure.

persons.name: Mary Schapiro
persons.bluf: The career regulator (CFTC, SEC commissioner, FINRA CEO) who rebuilt the SEC after the Madoff/Cox debacle. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence her money-market-fund reform fight — which she lost at the SEC but won later at FSOC — exposed a structural fragility re-confirmed in 2020.
persons.drivers: Institutional-repair mission; deep regulatory pedigree; investor-protection focus.
persons.worldview: Pragmatic regulator; saw money funds and market structure as systemic blind spots.
persons.decision_style: Methodical rebuilder; pushed reform against industry resistance and a divided commission.
persons.track_record: Post-crisis enforcement ramp-up; Dodd-Frank implementation; money-market-fund reform (blocked 3-2, later advanced via FSOC); flash-crash response.
persons.vulnerabilities: Lost the initial MMF vote; later joined Promontory/Bloomberg (revolving door).
persons.relationships: FINRA/SRO lineage; her MMF warnings vindicated in the March-2020 dash-for-cash.
persons.outlook: On-thesis figure for money-market/short-term-funding fragility.

persons.name: Mary Jo White
persons.bluf: The former SDNY US Attorney (she prosecuted Gotti and the 1993 WTC bombers) who brought 'broken windows' enforcement and admissions-of-wrongdoing to the SEC. Assess with MODERATE confidence her Debevoise corporate-defense background and recusals embodied the revolving-door tension she was hired to transcend.
persons.drivers: Prosecutor's enforcement instinct; reputation for toughness; corporate-bar pragmatism.
persons.worldview: Credible deterrence requires admissions and visible cases, but within a market-functioning frame.
persons.decision_style: Aggressive on small infractions ('broken windows'), selective on the largest firms; numerous recusals from former clients.
persons.track_record: Record penalties; admissions policy; market-structure (Reg SCI, tick-size pilot); but criticized for under-charging executives.
persons.vulnerabilities: Debevoise client conflicts; the gap between rhetoric and big-bank accountability.
persons.relationships: SDNY prosecutorial lineage (shared with Comey, Bharara); corporate-defense revolving door.
persons.outlook: Historical exemplar of the prosecutor-defense-bar duality at the SEC.

persons.name: Jay Clayton
persons.bluf: The Sullivan & Cromwell deal lawyer who ran a capital-formation-friendly SEC, cracked down on ICOs, and framed Bitcoin/Ether as non-securities while suing token issuers. Assess with HIGH confidence his ICO posture set the contested securities-vs-commodity boundary that still governs crypto enforcement; now SDNY US Attorney.
persons.drivers: Transactional-lawyer's pro-capital-formation worldview; institutional caution on retail harm.
persons.worldview: Public markets should be attractive; fraud and unregistered offerings policed, but innovation accommodated.
persons.decision_style: Pragmatic, business-fluent; selective crypto enforcement (ICOs yes, BTC/ETH spot no).
persons.track_record: ICO enforcement wave (2017–18); Reg-A/crowdfunding expansion; declined to call Bitcoin a security; later returned as SDNY US Attorney (2025).
persons.vulnerabilities: S&C bank-client background; the unresolved token-classification ambiguity he left behind.
persons.relationships: Corporate-bar pipeline; predecessor to Gensler's harder crypto line and Atkins' reversal.
persons.outlook: On-thesis origin of the crypto securities-status fight central to the digital-asset blocks.

persons.name: Gary Gensler
persons.bluf: Goldman partner → CFTC swaps-reformer → MIT crypto lecturer → the crypto industry's chief adversary at the SEC. Assess with HIGH confidence his 'most tokens are securities' enforcement campaign (Coinbase, Binance, Ripple, Kraken suits) is the defining regulatory conflict of the digital-asset era the project tracks.
persons.drivers: Reformer's zeal turned against an industry he had studied; belief that crypto largely replicates unregistered securities; legacy-building.
persons.worldview: Markets need a cop; investor protection trumps innovation claims; disclosure is non-negotiable.
persons.decision_style: Aggressive, litigation-forward, expansive on jurisdiction; 'regulation by enforcement' is the standing critique.
persons.track_record: As CFTC chair, implemented Dodd-Frank swaps rules and pursued LIBOR cases; as SEC chair, climate-disclosure rule (stayed/withdrawn), T+1 settlement, and the sweeping crypto-enforcement docket; mixed court results (lost key Ripple points).
persons.vulnerabilities: Courts pushed back on jurisdictional overreach; the FTX collapse occurred on his watch despite his focus; ousted in the 2025 transition.
persons.relationships: Antagonist of Coinbase/Armstrong, Binance/CZ, Ripple/Garlinghouse; reversed by successor Atkins.
persons.outlook: On-thesis keystone of the crypto-regulation arc; his exit marked the pivot to accommodation.
persons.legitimacy_bearing: A cautionary case of legitimacy overdrawn: spent the regulator's trust bearing on an aggressive 'regulation by enforcement' crypto posture that courts partly rejected and the successor administration reversed.

persons.name: Paul Atkins
persons.bluf: The deregulatory former commissioner and crypto-industry adviser installed to reverse the Gensler agenda. Assess with HIGH confidence his tenure pivots the SEC toward digital-asset accommodation, tokenization, and lighter disclosure — a live policy inflection in the crypto/stablecoin arc.
persons.drivers: Free-market, cost-benefit ideology; long-standing crypto-industry ties (Token Alliance); deregulatory mission.
persons.worldview: Innovation should not be strangled by enforcement; clear rules over case-by-case litigation.
persons.decision_style: Rulemaking-over-enforcement; rolling back climate disclosure, easing crypto registration, encouraging tokenized securities.
persons.track_record: As 2002–08 commissioner, opposed large corporate penalties; post-government advised crypto/fintech firms; now steering the SEC's crypto 'reset.'
persons.vulnerabilities: Industry-adviser background invites the inverse-capture critique; deregulation tail risk if a digital-asset blowup follows.
persons.relationships: Counterpoint to Gensler; aligned with the Trump-administration crypto-accommodation posture (Sacks as AI/crypto czar).
persons.outlook: On-thesis driver of the current pro-crypto regulatory turn.
persons.legitimacy_bearing: Rebuilding SEC legitimacy on the opposite tack (dropping crypto cases) - trust bearing contingent on whether the deregulation reads as principle or as capture in the Fairshake-spending era.

persons.name: Brooksley Born
persons.bluf: The Cassandra of OTC derivatives. Assess with HIGH confidence her 1998 concept release warning about unregulated swaps was crushed by the Greenspan-Rubin-Summers bloc and the Commodity Futures Modernization Act (2000) — a decision vindicated catastrophically in 2008. The single clearest case study of regulatory capture defeating prescient oversight.
persons.drivers: Lawyer's duty to surface hidden systemic risk; refusal to defer to the prevailing deregulation consensus.
persons.worldview: Opaque, unregulated derivatives markets are a systemic time bomb requiring transparency and oversight.
persons.decision_style: Principled, persistent, willing to be isolated; pressed the concept release despite intense pressure.
persons.track_record: Her May-1998 OTC-derivatives concept release was met with a coordinated effort to strip the CFTC's authority; Congress imposed a moratorium and then exempted swaps via the CFMA; she resigned in 1999. The 2008 crisis proved her right.
persons.vulnerabilities: Out-powered politically; the establishment treated her warning as a threat to market 'innovation.'
persons.relationships: Antagonized by Greenspan, Rubin, and Summers; the defining adversary trio of the capture thesis (PBS 'The Warning').
persons.outlook: Permanent on-thesis reference: the regulator who was right and ignored.

persons.name: Timothy Massad
persons.bluf: The ex-Treasury TARP overseer who implemented Dodd-Frank's swaps regime and began grappling with crypto and clearinghouse risk. Assess with MODERATE confidence his clearing-mandate work hardened post-2008 derivatives plumbing while concentrating risk in central counterparties.
persons.drivers: Implementation-technocrat's diligence; financial-stability orientation.
persons.worldview: Central clearing and transparency reduce systemic risk if CCPs are themselves resilient.
persons.decision_style: Methodical rule-finisher; pragmatic on cross-border harmonization.
persons.track_record: Completed swaps clearing/reporting rules; early CFTC attention to Bitcoin as a commodity; CCP-resilience focus.
persons.vulnerabilities: CCP concentration is the unresolved systemic question his regime created.
persons.relationships: Treasury-TARP lineage (Geithner era); predecessor to the crypto-forward Giancarlo.
persons.outlook: Historical builder of the cleared-derivatives architecture.

persons.name: J. Christopher Giancarlo
persons.bluf: 'CryptoDad' — the chair who let Bitcoin futures launch (Dec 2017) and now leads the Digital Dollar Project advocating a US CBDC. Assess with HIGH confidence he personally bridges the crypto-derivatives and central-bank-digital-currency arcs the project tracks as convergent control rails.
persons.drivers: Market-structure innovator's enthusiasm; belief the US must lead digital-asset and digital-currency design or cede it.
persons.worldview: 'Do no harm' to innovation; a sovereign digital dollar is preferable to ceding the rail to China or stablecoins.
persons.decision_style: Forward-leaning, media-savvy; approved CME/Cboe Bitcoin futures via self-certification.
persons.track_record: Bitcoin-futures launch; 'Crypto Dad' Senate testimony; post-CFTC founded the Digital Dollar Project (CBDC research) and joined crypto boards (BlockFi, etc.).
persons.vulnerabilities: CBDC advocacy collides with surveillance/privacy concerns central to the digital-ID thesis; crypto-board ties raise revolving-door questions.
persons.relationships: Node connecting crypto-derivatives, CBDC design, and the digital-ID/control-rail debate.
persons.outlook: On-thesis figure linking the crypto and CBDC layers.

persons.name: Heath Tarbert
persons.bluf: The chair who declared Ether a commodity, then moved to Circle (USDC issuer) as chief legal officer. Assess with HIGH confidence his ETH ruling and stablecoin-industry move place him directly on the regulator-to-crypto revolving-door and the stablecoin-rail throughline.
persons.drivers: Legal-scholar's classification rigor; pro-innovation regulatory philosophy; career mobility into the industry he regulated.
persons.worldview: Clear asset classification unlocks markets; the US should set digital-asset standards.
persons.decision_style: Doctrinal, clarity-seeking; the 'Ether is a commodity' statement settled a key jurisdictional question.
persons.track_record: ETH-commodity determination; LabCFTC fintech engagement; then Circle CLO and later Circle president — at the center of the USDC/stablecoin-Treasury rail.
persons.vulnerabilities: The direct CFTC-to-stablecoin-issuer move is a textbook revolving-door optic.
persons.relationships: Connects CFTC classification to the Circle/Allaire stablecoin node in the bubble map.
persons.outlook: On-thesis connector of derivatives classification and the stablecoin rail.

persons.name: Rostin Behnam
persons.bluf: The chair who pushed to make the CFTC the lead US crypto-spot regulator — and who met repeatedly with Sam Bankman-Fried as FTX lobbied for a friendly framework before its collapse. Assess with HIGH confidence the FTX episode is a cautionary node in the crypto-capture narrative.
persons.drivers: Jurisdiction-building ambition for the CFTC; belief that commodity-style oversight fits crypto better than securities law.
persons.worldview: The CFTC should be the digital-asset cop; principles-based regulation over enforcement.
persons.decision_style: Pro-engagement with industry; the FTX/DCCPA push later looked like proximity to a fraud.
persons.track_record: Advocated CFTC spot-crypto authority; multiple documented SBF meetings; climate-risk and clearing initiatives; left in the 2025 transition.
persons.vulnerabilities: The optics of FTX access pre-collapse; the unenacted FTX-favored legislation.
persons.relationships: Met repeatedly with Bankman-Fried (now a bubble-map person node); regulatory rival to Gensler's SEC over crypto turf.
persons.outlook: On-thesis cautionary figure for industry proximity in crypto policy.

persons.name: Caroline Pham
persons.bluf: The acting chair steering the CFTC's pro-crypto 'sprint' — tokenized collateral pilots and a lighter digital-asset posture aligned with the Trump-administration reset. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence she is advancing the regulatory accommodation that mirrors Atkins at the SEC.
persons.drivers: Market-structure modernization; deregulatory alignment with the administration; industry-engagement philosophy.
persons.worldview: Tokenization and clear crypto rules strengthen US market competitiveness.
persons.decision_style: Initiative-driven (the 'crypto sprint'); pro-pilot, pro-engagement.
persons.track_record: Tokenized-collateral and crypto-listing initiatives; enforcement-restraint posture during the 2025 transition.
persons.vulnerabilities: Same inverse-capture critique as the broader accommodation turn.
persons.relationships: CFTC counterpart to Atkins' SEC; aligned with the administration's digital-asset agenda.
persons.outlook: On-thesis driver of the 2025 crypto-accommodation pivot.

persons.name: William Isaac
persons.bluf: The FDIC chair during the Continental Illinois rescue (1984) — the failure that birthed the phrase 'too big to fail.' Assess with HIGH confidence his handling set the template for treating large-bank failures as systemic events warranting full backstops.
persons.drivers: Bank-stability mandate; pragmatism over moral-hazard purism when systemic risk loomed.
persons.worldview: Depositor confidence is paramount; large-bank runs threaten the whole system.
persons.decision_style: Interventionist in the crunch; later a vocal critic of mark-to-market accounting.
persons.track_record: Continental Illinois rescue (all depositors protected, TBTF coined by a congressman in the hearings); navigated the early-1980s bank failures.
persons.vulnerabilities: The TBTF precedent he helped set is a root of the moral-hazard critique.
persons.relationships: Origin node of the TBTF lineage that runs through 2008 to 2023.
persons.outlook: Historical anchor of the too-big-to-fail doctrine.

persons.name: L. William Seidman
persons.bluf: The cleanup architect of the savings-and-loan crisis, who ran both the FDIC and the Resolution Trust Corporation. Assess with HIGH confidence his RTC asset-disposition model is the template for resolving mass insolvencies — directly relevant to any future bank-failure wave.
persons.drivers: Problem-solver's pragmatism; public-service duty; willingness to clash with the administration over cost honesty.
persons.worldview: Bad assets must be recognized and sold, not hidden; transparency speeds recovery.
persons.decision_style: Hands-on, blunt about losses (clashed with the Bush White House over the S&L bailout's true cost).
persons.track_record: Resolved hundreds of failed thrifts; the RTC sold ~$400B in assets; later a CNBC commentator and frank critic of forbearance.
persons.vulnerabilities: His cost-honesty made him politically inconvenient.
persons.relationships: The resolution-model precedent invoked in every later crisis.
persons.outlook: On-thesis template for systemic bank-failure resolution.

persons.name: Sheila Bair
persons.bluf: The GFC-era FDIC chair who pushed mortgage modifications and clashed openly with Geithner and Paulson over bailouts she saw as favoring banks over homeowners. Assess with HIGH confidence she is the leading insider dissenter of the 2008 response — a key counter-narrative voice in the capture thesis.
persons.drivers: Consumer-protection conviction; institutional independence; willingness to be the skunk at the party.
persons.worldview: Bailouts should not reward the architects of the crisis; homeowners deserved parity with banks.
persons.decision_style: Combative within the inner circle; documented her objections in 'Bull by the Horns.'
persons.track_record: Raised deposit-insurance limits; resolved hundreds of bank failures; pushed systematic loan modifications resisted by Treasury; warned on subprime early.
persons.vulnerabilities: Outvoted by the Treasury/Fed axis on the bank-friendly tilt of the response.
persons.relationships: Antagonist of Geithner/Paulson; ally in spirit of the homeowner-relief camp.
persons.outlook: On-thesis dissenting voice documenting the banks-first resolution bias.

persons.name: Martin Gruenberg
persons.bluf: The long-serving FDIC technocrat who ran the 2023 regional-bank resolutions (SVB, Signature, First Republic) and the systemic-risk exception that backstopped uninsured depositors. Assess with HIGH confidence his 2023 handling is the most recent live test of the HTM-loss / deposit-flight fragility the project tracks. Resigned 2024 amid a toxic-workplace report.
persons.drivers: Institutional-continuity ethos; resolution-mechanics expertise; deference to systemic-stability calls.
persons.worldview: Orderly resolution and the deposit-insurance fund are the backstop of last resort.
persons.decision_style: Procedural, low-profile; invoked the systemic-risk exception in March 2023 to cover all SVB/Signature deposits.
persons.track_record: Basel III endgame proposal; 2023 bank failures and the uninsured-depositor backstop; long-running living-wills regime; resigned after the workplace-culture scandal.
persons.vulnerabilities: The workplace report ended his tenure; the 2023 backstop reignited moral-hazard and 'TBTF by precedent' debates.
persons.relationships: Worked with Yellen/Powell on the 2023 response; continuity through multiple administrations.
persons.outlook: On-thesis figure for the unrealized-loss / deposit-flight failure mode.

persons.name: Jelena McWilliams
persons.bluf: The Trump-appointed FDIC chair who courted fintech via industrial-loan-company charters and then resigned in an extraordinary board power struggle with CFPB director Chopra. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence the standoff exposed the contested governance of the bank-regulatory perimeter as fintech encroached.
persons.drivers: Deregulatory, pro-innovation orientation; immigrant-success narrative; institutional-prerogative defense.
persons.worldview: Regulation should enable new entrants; fintech and ILC charters expand competition.
persons.decision_style: Pro-charter, tailoring-focused; the 2021 board clash (over merger-review policy) was a rare public rupture.
persons.track_record: ILC charters (Square, Nelnet); 'tailoring' of post-crisis rules; resigned Feb 2022 after the Chopra/Gruenberg board maneuver.
persons.vulnerabilities: The power-struggle exit; fintech-charter expansion blurred the regulated perimeter.
persons.relationships: Antagonist of Chopra; predecessor to Gruenberg's return.
persons.outlook: Historical illustration of the fintech-perimeter governance fight.

persons.name: Travis Hill
persons.bluf: The acting FDIC chair reversing 'Operation Chokepoint 2.0' — easing bank engagement with crypto and unwinding the de-banking posture. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence he is implementing the administration's crypto-accommodation agenda at the deposit-insurance layer.
persons.drivers: Deregulatory alignment; tailoring philosophy; crypto-access advocacy.
persons.worldview: Banks should be free to serve lawful crypto clients; prior 'de-banking' was overreach.
persons.decision_style: Policy-reversal-focused; rescinding crypto-notification requirements, easing merger and tailoring rules.
persons.track_record: Withdrew restrictive crypto guidance; signaled lighter capital/merger posture; aligned with Atkins/Pham accommodation.
persons.vulnerabilities: Loosening at the deposit-insurance layer carries tail risk if a crypto-linked bank stress recurs (echoes of Signature/Silvergate).
persons.relationships: Part of the 2025 regulator cohort (Atkins, Pham, Gould) pivoting to crypto accommodation.
persons.outlook: On-thesis driver of the crypto-banking re-opening.

persons.name: Eugene Ludwig
persons.bluf: The Clinton-era comptroller who modernized the Community Reinvestment Act, then founded Promontory Financial Group — the consultancy that became the ultimate symbol of the regulatory revolving door. Assess with HIGH confidence his post-OCC career embodies the supervisory-expertise-for-hire model.
persons.drivers: Policy-entrepreneur's drive; belief that regulatory know-how is a marketable asset; CRA-modernization conviction.
persons.worldview: Banks and regulators share an interest in compliance infrastructure — which can be sold.
persons.decision_style: Innovative as comptroller; entrepreneurial after (Promontory advised the banks he once supervised).
persons.track_record: CRA reform; derivatives-supervision attention; then built Promontory (sold to IBM, 2016) and Ludwig Advisors/Canapi.
persons.vulnerabilities: Promontory is the canonical revolving-door case study; consent-order consulting drew scrutiny.
persons.relationships: OCC-to-consultancy pipeline; staffed Promontory with ex-regulators.
persons.outlook: On-thesis archetype of monetized supervisory expertise.

persons.name: John D. Hawke Jr.
persons.bluf: The comptroller who aggressively asserted federal preemption of state consumer-protection laws for national banks — building the legal shield that let the subprime machine operate beyond state reach. Assess with HIGH confidence his preemption doctrine is an under-appreciated structural enabler of the 2008 mortgage crisis.
persons.drivers: National-bank-charter primacy; federal-supremacy legal conviction; industry-aligned preemption push.
persons.worldview: National banks should answer to one federal regulator, not 50 state regimes.
persons.decision_style: Litigation-forward preemption; sidelined state AGs trying to police predatory lending.
persons.track_record: OCC preemption rules (2003–04) that blocked state anti-predatory-lending enforcement against national banks and their operating subsidiaries.
persons.vulnerabilities: Preemption removed a check that might have curbed subprime excess; later partially reversed by Dodd-Frank.
persons.relationships: Antagonist of state attorneys general; the preemption fight ran to the Supreme Court (Watters v. Wachovia).
persons.outlook: On-thesis structural enabler of the pre-2008 lending environment.

persons.name: John Dugan
persons.bluf: The comptroller through the GFC who later became chairman of Citigroup — a clean revolving-door arc. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his pre-crisis tenure reflected the light-touch supervision that missed the mortgage build-up, while his Citi chairmanship completed the regulator-to-megabank loop.
persons.drivers: Banking-lawyer's industry fluency; institutional caution; career mobility into the sector.
persons.worldview: Capital and supervision matter, but he shared the era's underestimation of mortgage/securitization risk.
persons.decision_style: Process-oriented; defended preemption; supported overdraft and capital frameworks later criticized.
persons.track_record: OCC through the crisis; continued Hawke's preemption stance; later Citigroup board and chairman (2019–).
persons.vulnerabilities: The OCC-to-Citi chairmanship is a direct capture-thesis exhibit.
persons.relationships: Completes the OCC→Citigroup loop (echoing Rubin's Citi tenure).
persons.outlook: On-thesis revolving-door node.

persons.name: Thomas Curry
persons.bluf: The post-crisis comptroller who tightened supervision (heightened standards, BSA/AML enforcement) and floated a national fintech charter. Assess with MODERATE confidence his fintech-charter proposal opened the still-unresolved question of how to regulate non-bank payment and crypto firms.
persons.drivers: Bank-examiner's prudential rigor; consumer-protection focus; modernization instinct.
persons.worldview: Strong supervision plus a path for responsible fintech entry.
persons.decision_style: Prudential, enforcement-active; the fintech-charter idea was cautious but precedent-setting.
persons.track_record: Heightened-standards guidelines; AML/sanctions cases; 2016 special-purpose national fintech charter proposal (litigated for years).
persons.vulnerabilities: The fintech charter triggered state-regulator lawsuits and remains contested.
persons.relationships: Predecessor to the crypto-charter pushes of Brooks; counterweight to deregulators.
persons.outlook: Historical opener of the fintech-charter debate.

persons.name: Joseph Otting
persons.bluf: The OneWest CEO (alongside Mnuchin) turned Trump-era comptroller who rewrote the Community Reinvestment Act over fellow regulators' objections. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his OneWest-to-OCC path and unilateral CRA overhaul are textbook revolving-door and deregulation exhibits.
persons.drivers: Banker's deregulatory conviction; OneWest/Mnuchin alliance; deal-execution mindset.
persons.worldview: CRA and post-crisis rules were overly burdensome; modernization should reduce compliance friction.
persons.decision_style: Unilateral (finalized the CRA rewrite without the Fed or FDIC); industry-aligned.
persons.track_record: 2020 CRA final rule (later rescinded); Volcker Rule simplification; OneWest foreclosure-era background.
persons.vulnerabilities: The OneWest foreclosure record and the go-it-alone CRA rule (subsequently reversed).
persons.relationships: OneWest partnership with Mnuchin connects him to the Treasury/Gulf-PE node.
persons.outlook: On-thesis revolving-door and deregulation case.

persons.name: Brian Brooks
persons.bluf: Coinbase chief legal officer → acting OCC head → briefly Binance.US CEO → Bitfury chairman: the most concentrated crypto-revolving-door figure in US bank regulation. Assess with HIGH confidence his OCC interpretive letters (stablecoin reserves, crypto custody, payment charters) deliberately opened the federal banking system to crypto.
persons.drivers: Crypto-industry conviction; first-mover institutional ambition; rapid career arbitrage between regulator and industry.
persons.worldview: Banks should custody crypto and use stablecoins; federal charters can normalize digital assets.
persons.decision_style: Fast, precedent-setting interpretive letters; 'payment charter' push; openly pro-crypto.
persons.track_record: OCC letters permitting national banks to custody crypto and hold stablecoin reserves; proposed a national payments charter; then Binance.US CEO (resigned in months) and Bitfury chair.
persons.vulnerabilities: The Coinbase→OCC→Binance arc is the starkest revolving-door optic in the dataset; his letters were later scrutinized/walked back.
persons.relationships: Directly ties the OCC to Armstrong/Coinbase and CZ/Binance nodes in the bubble map.
persons.outlook: On-thesis keystone of crypto's penetration of the federal banking perimeter.

persons.name: Michael Hsu
persons.bluf: The acting comptroller who reversed Brooks's crypto-permissive posture, warned of 'too big to manage,' and scrutinized bank mergers. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his caution made the OCC the most crypto-skeptical federal banking regulator of the early-2020s — the posture now being unwound.
persons.drivers: Prudential, financial-stability orientation (ex-Fed); skepticism of unmanaged scale and crypto contagion.
persons.worldview: Large banks can become unmanageable; crypto-bank linkages need guardrails.
persons.decision_style: Cautious, gate-keeping (required pre-approval for crypto activities); merger-skeptical.
persons.track_record: Walked back Brooks-era crypto letters (required non-objection); 'too big to manage' speeches; tougher merger review.
persons.vulnerabilities: Acting status limited durability; his guardrails are being reversed by the 2025 cohort (Gould).
persons.relationships: Counterweight to Brooks; predecessor to the crypto-friendly Gould.
persons.outlook: On-thesis exemplar of the regulatory-pendulum swing now reversing.

persons.name: Jonathan Gould
persons.bluf: The Senate-confirmed comptroller (ex-OCC chief counsel, ex-Bitfury chief legal) restoring crypto-friendly national-bank rules. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence he completes the 2025 regulator cohort re-opening the federal banking system to digital assets, reversing Hsu.
persons.drivers: Crypto-industry and OCC-insider background; deregulatory legal philosophy; charter-modernization aim.
persons.worldview: National banks should engage crypto under clear rules; prior caution was excessive.
persons.decision_style: Rule-restoring; reinstating permissive crypto interpretations and trust/payment-charter pathways.
persons.track_record: As OCC chief counsel (Brooks era) helped author the crypto letters; at Bitfury led legal; now comptroller advancing the accommodation agenda.
persons.vulnerabilities: Bitfury background mirrors the Brooks revolving-door critique; tail risk of re-opened crypto-bank linkages.
persons.relationships: Bitfury tie links him to Brooks; part of the Atkins/Pham/Hill 2025 cohort.
persons.outlook: On-thesis completion of the crypto-banking re-opening.

persons.name: Lyndon B. Johnson
persons.bluf: The 'just before' anchor of the Nixon shock. Assess with HIGH confidence his 'guns and butter' financing of Vietnam plus the Great Society — without commensurate taxation — drained US gold reserves and strained the Bretton Woods dollar-gold peg to the point that forced Nixon's 1971 closure.
persons.drivers: Legislative-mastery ambition; New Deal-liberal conviction; refusal to choose between war and domestic spending.
persons.worldview: Activist government can do both guns and butter; deficits are a manageable political tool.
persons.decision_style: Coercive legislative dealmaking ('the Johnson treatment'); escalation-prone in Vietnam.
persons.track_record: Great Society, Civil Rights/Voting Rights Acts, Medicare/Medicaid — financed alongside Vietnam, producing the gold drain and inflation that destabilized Bretton Woods.
persons.vulnerabilities: The fiscal contradiction he bequeathed; Vietnam's credibility collapse.
persons.relationships: Set the monetary trap his successor Nixon resolved by abandoning gold convertibility.
persons.outlook: Historical origin of the fiscal pressure that ended the gold-anchored system.

persons.name: Richard Nixon
persons.bluf: The president who detonated the shock that defines this entire project: on 15 Aug 1971 he closed the gold window, ending dollar-gold convertibility and launching the fiat era. Assess with HIGH confidence this single decision is the structural origin of the unanchored monetary system the investigation maps forward.
persons.drivers: Political survival (1972 re-election); geopolitical realism; resentment-fueled secrecy.
persons.worldview: Power-realist; saw monetary and foreign policy as instruments of national and personal advantage.
persons.decision_style: Strategic and secretive (the Camp David weekend was sprung without warning); willing to break the international order for domestic gain.
persons.track_record: Closed the gold window + imposed wage-price controls and an import surcharge (1971); opened China; détente with the USSR; resigned over Watergate (1974).
persons.vulnerabilities: Watergate; the controls failed and inflation embedded; paranoia and abuse of the intelligence apparatus.
persons.relationships: Enabled by Connally (Treasury) and Burns (Fed); the trio that ended Bretton Woods.
persons.outlook: The foundational node of the fiat-era thesis — everything downstream of 1971.

persons.name: Gerald Ford
persons.bluf: The unelected president who inherited stagflation and the post-shock dollar. Assess with MODERATE confidence his 'Whip Inflation Now' campaign exemplified the era's analytical helplessness before stagflation — a phenomenon the prevailing models could not explain.
persons.drivers: Institutional-healing duty (post-Watergate); fiscal-conservative instinct; consensus temperament.
persons.worldview: Moderate Republican; balanced-budget orientation; market-trusting.
persons.decision_style: Steady, conciliatory; the Nixon pardon defined his legitimacy debate.
persons.track_record: WIN program (widely mocked); refused to bail out New York City initially ('Ford to City: Drop Dead'); navigated recession and the oil-shock aftermath.
persons.vulnerabilities: The pardon; stagflation outran his tools; lost to Carter.
persons.relationships: Retained Burns at the Fed; bridge between the shock and the Volcker reckoning.
persons.outlook: Historical illustration of pre-Volcker inflation impotence.

persons.name: Jimmy Carter
persons.bluf: The president whose most consequential economic act was appointing Paul Volcker (1979) and tolerating the disinflation that cost him re-election. Assess with HIGH confidence his deregulation wave (airlines, trucking, rail, banking) reshaped the US economy more durably than his presidency's reputation suggests.
persons.drivers: Engineer's technocratic rigor; moral seriousness; willingness to accept political cost for sound policy.
persons.worldview: Reform-minded, anti-cartel deregulator; human-rights internationalist.
persons.decision_style: Detail-immersed, sometimes micromanaging; chose Volcker knowing the pain.
persons.track_record: Airline/trucking/rail deregulation; Depository Institutions Deregulation Act (1980); Volcker appointment; Iran hostage crisis and the second oil shock sank him.
persons.vulnerabilities: Inflation, hostages, and 'malaise' optics; banking deregulation seeded later S&L excess.
persons.relationships: Empowered Volcker's independence; antithesis of the 'guns and butter' fiscal style.
persons.outlook: On-thesis for the deregulation lineage and the restoration of Fed credibility.

persons.name: Ronald Reagan
persons.bluf: The president of supply-side economics, sweeping deregulation, and a tripling of the national debt. Assess with HIGH confidence 'Reaganomics' — tax cuts plus deregulation plus defense spending — set the template for deficit-financed growth and the deregulated-finance era that compounds into the current fiscal trap.
persons.drivers: Ideological anti-statism; Cold-War conviction; optimistic narrative discipline.
persons.worldview: Government is the problem; markets and tax cuts unleash growth; strength deters adversaries.
persons.decision_style: Big-picture delegation; backed Volcker's disinflation despite the recession; let deficits run for defense and tax cuts.
persons.track_record: 1981/1986 tax acts; financial and S&L deregulation (Garn-St Germain, 1982 — a direct cause of the S&L crisis); debt nearly tripled; Cold-War buildup.
persons.vulnerabilities: S&L deregulation blew up; deficits exploded; Iran-Contra.
persons.relationships: Backed Volcker; Donald Regan/Baker Treasury; the deregulation lineage to Clinton.
persons.outlook: On-thesis ancestor of deficit-financed growth and financial deregulation.

persons.name: George H.W. Bush
persons.bluf: The one-term president who cleaned up the S&L crisis (RTC) and broke his 'read my lips' tax pledge to address the deficit. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his S&L resolution is the template for socialized bank-failure cleanup, while the deficit deal cost him politically.
persons.drivers: Patrician public-service ethos; foreign-policy primacy; pragmatic fiscal realism.
persons.worldview: Prudent internationalist; deficits matter enough to break a pledge over.
persons.decision_style: Cautious, coalition-building (Gulf War); pragmatic on taxes despite the campaign promise.
persons.track_record: RTC/S&L cleanup; 1990 budget deal (tax increase); Gulf War; NAFTA negotiation; early-1990s recession.
persons.vulnerabilities: 'Read my lips' reversal; recession-driven loss to Clinton.
persons.relationships: Brady Treasury; Seidman RTC; the cleanup model invoked since.
persons.outlook: On-thesis for the socialized-bank-cleanup template.

persons.name: Bill Clinton
persons.bluf: The president of the 1990s boom — balanced budgets and the two deregulations most central to this investigation: the Glass-Steagall repeal (1999) and the Commodity Futures Modernization Act (2000). Assess with HIGH confidence those laws, driven by his Rubin/Summers Treasury, dismantled the firewalls whose absence defined 2008.
persons.drivers: Triangulation and political durability; growth-coalition building; faith in the New Economy.
persons.worldview: Market-friendly 'Third Way' centrism; deregulated finance as a growth engine.
persons.decision_style: Consensus-seeking, poll-attuned; deferred to the Rubin/Greenspan/Summers economic bloc.
persons.track_record: Deficit reduction and surpluses; NAFTA/WTO; welfare reform; Gramm-Leach-Bliley (Glass-Steagall repeal); CFMA (OTC-derivatives exemption that buried Born's warning).
persons.vulnerabilities: The two deregulations are central capture-thesis exhibits; later expressed partial regret on derivatives.
persons.relationships: Rubin/Summers Treasury; aligned with Greenspan; the bloc that beat Born.
persons.outlook: On-thesis keystone of the financial-deregulation chapter that set up 2008.

persons.name: George W. Bush
persons.bluf: The president of the 'ownership society' housing push, deficit-financed wars and tax cuts, and the 2008 crash that detonated in his final months. Assess with HIGH confidence the TARP backstop his administration enacted institutionalized too-big-to-fail as federal policy.
persons.drivers: Conviction-driven decisiveness; post-9/11 security primacy; pro-homeownership ideology.
persons.worldview: Compassionate-conservative + security hawk; expanding homeownership as social good.
persons.decision_style: Instinctive, loyalty-valuing; deferred to Paulson/Bernanke in the crisis ('this sucker could go down').
persons.track_record: 2001/2003 tax cuts; Iraq/Afghanistan wars (off-budget); homeownership push amid the bubble; TARP, AIG/Fannie/Freddie backstops (2008).
persons.vulnerabilities: Deficits, wars, and the crash define the economic legacy; the bailouts entrenched moral hazard.
persons.relationships: Paulson Treasury; Bernanke Fed; Cox SEC (the pre-crisis light touch).
persons.outlook: On-thesis for the crisis-backstop and TBTF-institutionalization lineage.

persons.name: Barack Obama
persons.bluf: The president of the post-GFC recovery, Dodd-Frank, and the ZIRP/QE asset-inflation decade. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence the bank-centric recovery (stimulus + stress tests + Fed liquidity) restored the system while widening the wealth gap and entrenching the asset-price-support regime — and his administration expanded the surveillance apparatus later exposed by Snowden.
persons.drivers: Pragmatic stabilization over structural reform; technocratic competence; institutional caution.
persons.worldview: Center-left reformer who chose system-repair over rupture; tech-optimist.
persons.decision_style: Deliberative, consensus-built, risk-averse on financial structure (kept the banks intact).
persons.track_record: ARRA stimulus; Dodd-Frank/CFPB; auto bailout; ACA; the QE/ZIRP era; drone and bulk-surveillance expansion (NSA programs Snowden disclosed in 2013).
persons.vulnerabilities: Few bankers prosecuted; foreclosure relief fell short; the surveillance expansion sits uneasily with civil-liberties critiques.
persons.relationships: Geithner/Lew Treasury; Bernanke/Yellen Fed; the bank-first resolution he ratified.
persons.outlook: On-thesis for the asset-inflation decade and the surveillance-state expansion.

persons.name: Joe Biden
persons.bluf: The president of the post-COVID stimulus, the CHIPS Act, and the export-control war on Chinese semiconductors. Assess with HIGH confidence his administration's CHIPS/export-control regime and large fiscal injections are direct drivers of both the AI-buildout the project maps and the inflation surge that followed.
persons.drivers: New Deal-scale ambition; industrial-policy revival; alliance-rebuilding; China-competition framing.
persons.worldview: Industrial-policy interventionist; reshoring strategic supply chains; sanctions/export controls as statecraft.
persons.decision_style: Coalition-built, institution-deferring; aggressive on China tech controls, slow to acknowledge inflation.
persons.track_record: American Rescue Plan (2021); CHIPS and Science Act (2022, semis reshoring); IRA; sweeping semiconductor export controls (Oct 2022 and after); AI Executive Order (2023); crypto-enforcement era; Russia-sanctions architecture.
persons.vulnerabilities: Inflation surge tied to the stimulus scale; sanctions overuse cited as a de-dollarization driver.
persons.relationships: Yellen Treasury; Powell Fed; the export-control regime that frames the chip-chokepoint blocks.
persons.outlook: On-thesis driver of the chip war, the AI-capex environment, and the fiscal/inflation backdrop.

persons.name: Marco Rubio
persons.bluf: The China-hawk senator turned Secretary of State, central to the technology-and-export-control front of US-China competition. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his tenure hardens the tech-decoupling and sanctions posture that frames the chip-chokepoint and contested-resource blocks.
persons.drivers: Cuban-exile anti-authoritarianism; great-power-competition framing; ambition and adaptation to Trump's orbit.
persons.worldview: China is the defining strategic adversary; technology, supply chains, and alliances are the battlespace.
persons.decision_style: Hawkish, alliance-and-sanctions-oriented; pragmatic realignment from rival to loyal principal.
persons.track_record: Senate sanctions and export-control advocacy (Huawei, Xinjiang); as State Secretary, leads the diplomatic side of decoupling, Latin America policy, and Gulf engagement.
persons.vulnerabilities: The pivot from Trump critic to cabinet loyalist; balancing decoupling against allied and market costs.
persons.relationships: Counterpart to Xi/Wang in the US-China contest; aligned with Greer on trade.
persons.outlook: On-thesis driver of the tech-decoupling and chip-war diplomacy.

persons.name: Jamieson Greer
persons.bluf: The Lighthizer protégé running US trade policy — the architect of the 2025 tariff regime and chip/technology export-control negotiations. Assess with HIGH confidence he is the operational point man for the trade-and-tech-restriction front central to the chip-chokepoint thesis.
persons.drivers: Economic-nationalist conviction; tariff-as-leverage doctrine (Lighthizer school); legal-precision craft.
persons.worldview: Tariffs and export controls are legitimate tools to reshore industry and contain rivals.
persons.decision_style: Legalistic, leverage-maximizing, hard-bargaining; comfortable with disruption as leverage.
persons.track_record: Lighthizer's USTR deputy in the first term (Section 301 China tariffs, USMCA); in 2025 leads the reciprocal-tariff rollout and semiconductor/critical-mineral trade negotiations.
persons.vulnerabilities: Tariff inflation and allied-relationship costs; litigation over tariff authority.
persons.relationships: Lighthizer lineage; works with Rubio/Bessent/Lutnick on the China-tech front.
persons.outlook: On-thesis driver of the trade-war and export-control dynamics.

persons.name: Russell Vought
persons.role: Director, Office of Management and Budget (2025–)
persons.bluf: The Project 2025 architect running OMB — the budget-and-administrative-control lever of the second Trump term. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his impoundment and administrative-restructuring agenda is the most consequential attempt to reshape executive control of spending and the regulatory state in decades.
persons.drivers: Ideological commitment to executive power and a smaller administrative state; Christian-nationalist-adjacent worldview; institutional-restructuring zeal.
persons.worldview: The president should control spending and the bureaucracy directly; impoundment is a legitimate tool.
persons.decision_style: Systematic, doctrine-driven (authored the budget/agency-restructuring blueprint); confrontational with Congress over appropriations.
persons.track_record: OMB director in the first term; Project 2025 lead author; in 2025 pursues impoundment, agency reductions, and Schedule F-style civil-service changes.
persons.vulnerabilities: Impoundment fights face legal and constitutional challenge (Impoundment Control Act).
persons.relationships: Ideological core of the administration's restructuring; works with the broader Project-2025 network.
persons.outlook: On-thesis for executive-control and fiscal-restructuring dynamics that shape the regulatory environment.

persons.name: Steve Witkoff
persons.bluf: The real-estate magnate turned special envoy handling the highest-stakes negotiations — Ukraine-Russia and the Middle East — with deep Gulf relationships. Assess with MODERATE confidence his dealmaker profile and Gulf ties connect the diplomatic track to the Gulf-sovereign-AI-capital node the project maps.
persons.drivers: Transactional dealmaker's instinct; personal loyalty to Trump; access-and-relationship capital in the Gulf.
persons.worldview: Geopolitics as negotiable deals; personal rapport over institutional process.
persons.decision_style: Improvisational, relationship-driven, back-channel-heavy; unconventional for high diplomacy.
persons.track_record: Manhattan real-estate career; in 2025 led Gaza-ceasefire and Russia-Ukraine back-channels and Gulf engagement.
persons.vulnerabilities: Lack of diplomatic institutional grounding; conflict optics between private Gulf ties and official negotiations.
persons.relationships: Gulf-leadership rapport (links to MBS/Tahnoon nodes); Putin/Zelensky negotiation counterpart.
persons.outlook: On-thesis connector of high diplomacy and Gulf capital.

persons.name: John Ratcliffe
persons.role: Director, CIA (2025–); Director of National Intelligence (2020–2021)
persons.bluf: The only person to serve as both DNI and CIA Director, and a Trump loyalist atop the foreign-intelligence apparatus. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his tenure reorients CIA toward China-competition and the administration's priorities, with declassification used as a political instrument.
persons.drivers: Partisan loyalty; China-threat conviction; prosecutorial assertiveness (ex-US Attorney).
persons.worldview: Intelligence should be unsentimental about China and responsive to the elected executive.
persons.decision_style: Aggressive, declassification-willing, loyalty-aligned.
persons.track_record: Congressman → DNI (2020–21, contested declassifications) → CIA Director (2025), refocusing on China and covert-action priorities.
persons.vulnerabilities: Politicization critiques; the tension between loyalty and analytic independence.
persons.relationships: Part of the intelligence-leadership cohort with Gabbard (DNI) and Patel (FBI).
persons.outlook: On-thesis node in the intelligence-apparatus realignment.

persons.name: Kash Patel
persons.bluf: The loyalist 'deep state' antagonist installed atop the FBI — a sharp break from the bureau's traditional independence. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his tenure reorients domestic intelligence/enforcement toward the administration's priorities, with significant civil-liberties and institutional-norm implications.
persons.drivers: Anti-establishment conviction; personal loyalty; grievance against the prior investigative apparatus.
persons.worldview: The federal investigative bureaucracy was weaponized and must be purged and redirected.
persons.decision_style: Disruptive, loyalty-driven, norm-breaking; promised structural overhaul.
persons.track_record: NSC/DoD roles in the first term; author of a 'deep state' critique; FBI Director (2025) pursuing leadership turnover and reorientation.
persons.vulnerabilities: Politicization and independence concerns; surveillance-authority stewardship under scrutiny.
persons.relationships: Intelligence cohort with Ratcliffe (CIA) and Gabbard (DNI); domestic-surveillance node.
persons.outlook: On-thesis for the domestic-surveillance and institutional-norm dimension.

persons.name: Pam Bondi
persons.bluf: The Florida ex-AG and Trump loyalist running DOJ, including the dramatic rollback of crypto-enforcement priorities. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence her disbanding of the crypto-enforcement unit and shift in prosecutorial posture materially eased the legal pressure on the digital-asset industry.
persons.drivers: Loyalty and prosecutorial-political alignment; law-and-order framing; industry-deregulatory posture on crypto.
persons.worldview: DOJ should pursue the administration's enforcement priorities; prior crypto cases were overreach.
persons.decision_style: Directive, priority-shifting; disbanded the National Cryptocurrency Enforcement Team (2025).
persons.track_record: Florida AG; lobbyist; AG (2025) — crypto-enforcement rollback, immigration/enforcement focus, leadership changes at DOJ.
persons.vulnerabilities: Politicization critiques; the crypto-enforcement reversal's tail risk if fraud resurges.
persons.relationships: Aligned with the Atkins/Pham/Hill crypto-accommodation cohort on the enforcement side.
persons.outlook: On-thesis driver of the crypto-enforcement retreat.

persons.name: Kristi Noem
persons.bluf: The DHS secretary overseeing border enforcement, REAL-ID implementation, and the federal identity/surveillance infrastructure. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence her department is the operational center of the US digital-identity and border-surveillance apparatus the project tracks.
persons.drivers: Border-security hard-line; loyalty and political ambition; enforcement-visibility orientation.
persons.worldview: Strong borders and verified identity are security imperatives.
persons.decision_style: Aggressive, media-forward enforcement; REAL-ID and biometric-screening expansion.
persons.track_record: South Dakota governor; DHS Secretary (2025) — REAL-ID enforcement, expanded biometric/identity screening (CBP, TSA), immigration crackdown.
persons.vulnerabilities: Civil-liberties and mission-creep concerns around federal identity and biometric systems.
persons.relationships: DHS is the US node of the digital-ID/surveillance convergence (links to the digital-ID blocks).
persons.outlook: On-thesis operator of the US digital-identity and surveillance layer.

persons.name: Stephen Miller
persons.role: White House Deputy Chief of Staff for Policy; Homeland Security Advisor (2025–)
persons.bluf: The most influential policy architect of the second term — immigration, homeland-security, and the enforcement-and-identity agenda. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence he is the central driver of the surveillance-and-identity policy stack, operating with unusual concentrated authority.
persons.drivers: Ideological restrictionism; institutional-leverage mastery; long-game policy persistence.
persons.worldview: Aggressive immigration enforcement and identity verification are core state functions.
persons.decision_style: Bureaucracy-savvy, relentless, detail-driven; concentrates policy control in the West Wing.
persons.track_record: First-term immigration architect (travel ban, family separation); 2025 deputy CoS for policy directing enforcement, identity, and homeland-security agendas.
persons.vulnerabilities: Civil-liberties controversy; concentration-of-power critiques.
persons.relationships: Works through Noem's DHS and Patel's FBI; node of the identity/surveillance policy stack.
persons.outlook: On-thesis architect of the surveillance-and-identity policy direction.

persons.name: Doug Burgum
persons.role: US Secretary of the Interior; Chair, National Energy Dominance Council (2025–)
persons.bluf: The software-entrepreneur governor turned Interior Secretary and energy-policy chief — the official tasked with powering the AI-data-center buildout. Assess with HIGH confidence his energy-dominance mandate sits directly on the AI-power-grid bottleneck the project identifies as a binding constraint on the buildout.
persons.drivers: Tech-executive's systems thinking; energy-abundance conviction; pro-growth, pro-buildout orientation.
persons.worldview: Cheap, abundant energy is the foundation of AI and economic dominance; permitting is the bottleneck to break.
persons.decision_style: Business-pragmatic, deregulatory on permitting; coordinates federal energy/data-center siting.
persons.track_record: Microsoft executive (Great Plains); North Dakota governor; 2025 Interior Secretary + National Energy Dominance Council chair — accelerating power generation, transmission, and land access for data centers.
persons.vulnerabilities: Water, grid, and community-siting constraints; the physical limits the buildout narrative underrates.
persons.relationships: Bridges the administration to the AI-infrastructure operators (links to the power/water/data-center blocks).
persons.outlook: On-thesis operator of the energy side of the AI buildout.

persons.name: Susie Wiles
persons.bluf: The disciplined campaign architect who became the first woman White House Chief of Staff and the operational gatekeeper of the second term. Assess with MODERATE confidence her process-discipline is the stabilizing counterweight in an otherwise disruption-oriented administration.
persons.drivers: Operational excellence; loyalty and discretion; behind-the-scenes power preference.
persons.worldview: Campaign-operative pragmatism; execution over ideology.
persons.decision_style: Organized, low-drama, gatekeeping; 'the Ice Maiden' reputation for control.
persons.track_record: Florida political operative; ran the 2024 campaign; CoS (2025) imposing unusual message and access discipline.
persons.vulnerabilities: The limits of discipline over a principal and cabinet inclined to disruption.
persons.relationships: Central node controlling West Wing access and flow.
persons.outlook: Process anchor of the administration.

persons.name: Robert F. Kennedy Jr.
persons.role: US Secretary of Health and Human Services (2025–)
persons.bluf: The environmental-lawyer and vaccine-skeptic scion running HHS under the 'Make America Healthy Again' banner. Assess with MODERATE confidence his tenure reshapes federal public-health, pharmaceutical, and health-data policy in ways that intersect the broader institutional-trust and data-governance themes.
persons.drivers: Anti-establishment conviction; dynastic identity reframed against institutions; crusading temperament.
persons.worldview: Chronic disease and institutional capture (pharma, food) are the real health crises; orthodoxy is suspect.
persons.decision_style: Combative, heterodox, personality-driven; restructures agencies and advisory committees.
persons.track_record: Environmental litigation; anti-vaccine advocacy; independent 2024 run; HHS Secretary (2025) — vaccine-policy and FDA/CDC restructuring, food/chronic-disease focus.
persons.vulnerabilities: Scientific-consensus conflicts; public-health-credibility and data-integrity disputes.
persons.relationships: Part of the heterodox wing; intersects health-data and institutional-trust themes.
persons.outlook: Adjacent-thesis figure on institutional trust and data governance.

persons.name: William O. Douglas
persons.bluf: The longest-serving justice and a former SEC chairman — a direct judiciary-to-financial-regulation node. Assess with HIGH confidence his New Deal securities-law foundation and expansive privacy/liberty jurisprudence (Griswold 'penumbras') shaped both market regulation and the privacy doctrine the surveillance debate now contests.
persons.drivers: New Deal reformism; civil-libertarian conviction; institutional iconoclasm.
persons.worldview: Concentrated financial power must be checked; individual privacy and liberty are constitutionally protected.
persons.decision_style: Bold, sometimes solitary; prolific dissenter; impatient with corporate prerogative.
persons.track_record: As SEC chair (1937–39) built early market regulation; on the Court, authored Griswold's privacy 'penumbras'; retired 1975.
persons.vulnerabilities: Personal controversies; impeachment attempts; idiosyncratic late tenure.
persons.relationships: The SEC-to-bench precedent; intellectual ancestor of the privacy doctrine.
persons.outlook: Historical anchor linking securities regulation and constitutional privacy.

persons.name: Warren E. Burger
persons.role: Chief Justice of the United States (1969–1986)
persons.bluf: The chief justice through the Nixon-shock era who, despite a law-and-order reputation, presided over a Court that ruled against executive overreach. Assess with HIGH confidence US v. Nixon (1974) — ordering the tapes' release — established that not even the president is above judicial process, a foundational executive-power limit.
persons.drivers: Institutional propriety; administrative-efficiency focus; law-and-order instinct moderated by rule-of-law duty.
persons.worldview: Order and process; courts as guardians of constitutional limits even against the executive that appointed them.
persons.decision_style: Managerial, coalition-managing; assigned himself the unanimous Nixon opinion.
persons.track_record: US v. Nixon (1974, 8-0); presided over Roe, the death-penalty cases, and early administrative-law doctrine.
persons.vulnerabilities: Mixed jurisprudential legacy; overshadowed by his successors' doctrinal revolutions.
persons.relationships: Confronted the president (Nixon) who elevated the Court's era.
persons.outlook: Historical anchor of executive-power limits.

persons.name: William J. Brennan Jr.
persons.bluf: The liberal coalition-builder who shaped late-20th-century rights jurisprudence. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his expansive due-process and free-expression doctrine underlies the speech and privacy frameworks that govern today's platform and surveillance debates.
persons.drivers: Rights-expansion conviction; the craft of assembling five votes; humane pragmatism.
persons.worldview: The Constitution is a living charter of expanding dignity and liberty.
persons.decision_style: Master vote-counter; persuasive consensus-builder.
persons.track_record: Free-speech, due-process, and apportionment landmarks across 34 years.
persons.vulnerabilities: His living-constitution method became the central target of originalist reaction.
persons.relationships: Intellectual antithesis of Scalia's originalism.
persons.outlook: Historical foundation of the rights framework later contested.

persons.name: Potter Stewart
persons.bluf: The pragmatic centrist best known for 'I know it when I see it,' and for Katz v. US (1967), which built the 'reasonable expectation of privacy' test. Assess with HIGH confidence that test is the doctrinal core of every modern digital-surveillance and Fourth-Amendment dispute.
persons.drivers: Case-by-case pragmatism; aversion to grand theory; institutional moderation.
persons.worldview: Narrow, fact-bound judging over sweeping doctrine.
persons.decision_style: Swing-vote pragmatism; quotable concision.
persons.track_record: Katz v. US privacy test; death-penalty and obscenity cases; retired 1981 (O'Connor succeeded).
persons.vulnerabilities: His reluctance to theorize left doctrine for others to systematize.
persons.relationships: Katz framework now stretched by digital-tracking cases (Carpenter etc.).
persons.outlook: On-thesis ancestor of the privacy-expectation doctrine.

persons.name: Byron White
persons.bluf: The Kennedy appointee and lone deference-minded centrist who often sided with government and against expansive privacy claims. Assess with MODERATE confidence his Bowers v. Hardwick and law-enforcement-friendly votes mark the conservative boundary of the era's privacy jurisprudence.
persons.drivers: Restraint-minded pragmatism; deference to legislatures and police; athlete's competitiveness.
persons.worldview: Judicial modesty; skeptical of unenumerated rights.
persons.decision_style: Independent, unpredictable, deference-leaning.
persons.track_record: Dissented in Miranda and Roe; authored Bowers (1986); securities and antitrust opinions.
persons.vulnerabilities: On the losing side of the rights-expansion arc he resisted.
persons.relationships: Counterweight to the Brennan wing.
persons.outlook: Historical marker of the deference tradition.

persons.name: Thurgood Marshall
persons.bluf: The civil-rights titan and first Black justice, an unwavering voice for the powerless against concentrated power. Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence his dissents on access-to-justice and equal protection frame the structural-inequality critique the project's distributional themes echo.
persons.drivers: Justice for the marginalized; lived experience of structural inequality; moral clarity.
persons.worldview: Law must protect the vulnerable from concentrated public and private power.
persons.decision_style: Storyteller-advocate; powerful dissents as future blueprints.
persons.track_record: Architect of Brown as an advocate; on the Court, defended affirmative action, due process, and the indigent.
persons.vulnerabilities: Increasingly in dissent as the Court moved right.
persons.relationships: Liberal bloc with Brennan; succeeded by Thomas (a sharp ideological reversal).
persons.outlook: Historical conscience on concentrated-power inequality.

persons.name: Harry Blackmun
persons.bluf: The Nixon appointee who authored Roe and drifted leftward over 24 years. Assess with MODERATE confidence his evolution illustrates how appointments defy the appointing president's expectations — a recurring theme in Court composition.
persons.drivers: Medical-law meticulousness (ex-Mayo Clinic counsel); growing empathy; institutional conscience.
persons.worldview: Evolved from restraint toward rights-protection and anti-death-penalty conviction.
persons.decision_style: Painstaking, data-driven; famously agonized over decisions.
persons.track_record: Roe v. Wade (1973); late-career 'I shall no longer tinker with the machinery of death' on capital punishment.
persons.vulnerabilities: Roe's contested foundation; criticized from both sides.
persons.relationships: Drifted from the Burger bloc toward Brennan/Marshall.
persons.outlook: Historical example of jurisprudential drift.

persons.name: Lewis F. Powell Jr.
persons.bluf: The corporate lawyer-justice whose pre-bench 1971 'Powell Memo' is a foundational text of the modern business-political mobilization. Assess with HIGH confidence both the memo and his Bellotti (1978) opinion expanding corporate political speech are direct ancestors of the corporate-power and money-in-politics arc running to Citizens United.
persons.drivers: Corporate-establishment defense; institutional moderation on the bench; belief business was under ideological siege.
persons.worldview: Free enterprise must organize politically to defend itself; pragmatic centrism in adjudication.
persons.decision_style: Swing-vote moderation; case-by-case balancing (Bakke).
persons.track_record: The Powell Memo (1971) galvanized the Chamber of Commerce and business advocacy; First National Bank v. Bellotti (1978) protected corporate political spending; Bakke compromise on affirmative action.
persons.vulnerabilities: The memo's legacy as a blueprint for corporate political influence is double-edged.
persons.relationships: Doctrinal ancestor of the Roberts-Court campaign-finance line.
persons.outlook: On-thesis origin node of organized corporate political power.

persons.name: William Rehnquist
persons.role: Chief Justice of the United States (1986–2005); Associate Justice (1972–1986)
persons.bluf: The architect of the modern federalism revival and a narrower commerce power. Assess with HIGH confidence his federalism doctrine (Lopez, Morrison) and states'-rights revival reshaped the constitutional limits on federal regulatory authority — the backdrop against which agency power is now contested.
persons.drivers: Conservative constitutional conviction; institutional leadership; intellectual consistency.
persons.worldview: Limited federal power; robust states' rights; skeptical of expansive commerce-clause regulation.
persons.decision_style: Efficient chief; coalition-leading; doctrinally disciplined.
persons.track_record: US v. Lopez (1995) and Morrison (2000) curbed commerce power; presided over Bush v. Gore (2000); built the conservative majority's federalism line.
