persons.vulnerabilities: The Labour Together donor-reporting fine; Conservative claims (from leaked emails) of an attempted cover-up framed as an admin error (contested; the Electoral Commission found no evidence of offenses and declined to reopen); the CCDH co-founding ties him to the contested content-moderation nexus.
persons.relationships: Gatekeeper to Starmer; co-founded CCDH with Imran Ahmed (linking the Labour, donor, and content-moderation strands); intersects the donor and digital-ID maps.
persons.outlook: A node controlling access and strategy in the Starmer government.
persons.grade: fact (roles, fine, CCDH co-founding) + contested (cover-up allegation)

persons.name: Peter Mandelson
persons.role: Former UK Ambassador to the United States (2025, sacked); Labour grandee; ex-EU Trade Commissioner; founder, Global Counsel
persons.bluf: A New Labour architect and consummate networker appointed UK ambassador to Washington in 2025 and SACKED months later (11 Sep 2025) after the US House Oversight Committee's release of Jeffrey Epstein's 'birthday book' and emails exposed a far deeper Epstein friendship than disclosed - including a letter calling Epstein his 'best pal' and messages of support after Epstein's 2008 conviction. Assess with HIGH confidence on the sacking and the Epstein correspondence; combined with his Global Counsel commercial ties (Russia/China/Gulf), he is the roster's clearest case of conflict-and-association scrutiny.
persons.drivers: Influence at the center of UK/EU/US power; commercial advisory; transatlantic relations.
persons.worldview: Pro-market New Labour internationalist; comfortable bridging politics and business.
persons.decision_style: Networked, strategic, controversy-weathering (multiple past resignations).

persons.vulnerabilities: The Epstein correspondence and sacking (documented via House Oversight + UK government); the Global Counsel client list and past associations with controversial financiers and foreign actors feed sustained conflict/foreign-ties scrutiny; a history of forced resignations.
persons.relationships: Blair-era ally; bridges UK politics, the EU, the US, and global commercial interests; the Epstein link connects him to the Epstein-document thread; Starmer appointed (then sacked) him.
persons.outlook: A node connecting UK influence, transatlantic diplomacy, and commercial advisory.
persons.confidence: MODERATE-HIGH (career documented); specific conflict claims graded case-by-case.
persons.grade: fact (sacking + Epstein emails documented) + labeled interpretation (commercial-conflict scrutiny)

persons.name: Alexander Bortnikov
persons.role: Director of the FSB (Russian Federal Security Service), in post since 2008
persons.bluf: Longest-serving chief of Russia's domestic security service and a core silovik in Putin's power vertical. The FSB under him is the most powerful of the intelligence triad, focused on internal control, counterintelligence, and regime protection — but the triad competes rather than coordinates (composition guard: institutional reach is not a single omniscient plan).
persons.drivers: Regime survival and internal stability; personal position within the silovik elite; continuity of the KGB/FSB institutional lineage that staffs the vertical.
persons.worldview: Interpretation: a security-service worldview treating internal dissent, foreign influence, and Western intelligence as existential threats to the state, justifying an expansive domestic-control mandate.
persons.decision_style: Interpretation: institutionally cautious, control-oriented, long-tenured insider operating through the FSB apparatus rather than as a public figure. Thinly documented at the individual level.
persons.track_record: In post since 2008 — the anchor of institutional continuity across Putin's later terms. The FSB's domestic primacy and counterintelligence role are well established; specific personal operational decisions are largely opaque.
persons.vulnerabilities: Bound to the fortunes of the power vertical and the war; sanctioned by Western governments; his standing depends on Putin's favor and on the FSB not visibly failing at internal security.
persons.relationships: Rival-peer to Kostyukov (GRU) and Naryshkin (SVR) within the competing triad; a member of the inner silovik circle around Putin alongside figures like Sechin.
persons.outlook: Interpretation: likely continuity in role so long as the vertical holds; the FSB's domestic-repression and counterintelligence weight grows as the war economy strains the state.
persons.legitimacy_bearing: Holds formal state office as FSB director; his authority is legal-institutional within the Russian state, though its exercise is widely contested internationally and under sanctions.
persons.confidence: Moderate — office, tenure, and institutional role are firmly grounded in the research file and public record; individual-level psychology and specific decisions are interpretation.
persons.grade: Solid on role/tenure/institutional position; weak/interpretive on personal drivers and decision style.

persons.name: Igor Kostyukov
persons.role: Chief of the GRU (Russian military intelligence, GU/Main Directorate of the General Staff), in post since 2018
persons.bluf: Head of Russian military intelligence, which the research ties to the most aggressive cyber and sabotage tracks — APT28/Fancy Bear and Sandworm. The GRU is the operational, risk-tolerant leg of the competing triad; grounding attributions to it sharpens the cyber-layer picture (composition guard: the services compete, not coordinate).
persons.drivers: Military-intelligence mission delivery; battlefield and hybrid-war advantage; institutional standing of the GRU within the armed forces and the vertical.
persons.worldview: Interpretation: a military-operational, offense-tolerant view of intelligence as an instrument of active measures, sabotage, and cyber effects in a confrontation with NATO/the West.
persons.decision_style: Interpretation: comparatively risk-tolerant and operationally aggressive relative to the SVR/FSB, consistent with the GRU's documented track record of bold, sometimes exposed operations. Thinly documented personally.
persons.track_record: GRU chief since 2018; the directorate is credibly linked to APT28 (Fancy Bear) and Sandworm and to a pattern of high-tempo cyber and physical operations. Personal-level decisions largely opaque.
persons.vulnerabilities: Western sanctions; the GRU's reputation for operational exposure (burned officers, attributed hacks) is an institutional liability; dependent on Putin's and the military leadership's confidence.
persons.relationships: Rival-peer to Bortnikov (FSB) and Naryshkin (SVR); embedded in the General Staff and defense establishment.
persons.outlook: Interpretation: continued high tempo of GRU cyber/sabotage activity while the war persists; the GRU is central to Russia's hybrid pressure on NATO states.
persons.legitimacy_bearing: Holds formal military-state office; authority is legal-institutional within Russia's armed forces, though its operations are widely contested and sanctioned abroad.
persons.confidence: Moderate — role, tenure, and the GRU/APT28/Sandworm linkage are grounded; individual psychology and specific attributions to him personally are interpretation.
persons.grade: Solid on role and institutional cyber linkage; interpretive on personal traits.

persons.name: Sergey Naryshkin
persons.role: Director of the SVR (Russian foreign intelligence service), in post since 2016
persons.bluf: Head of Russia's foreign intelligence service, which the research links to APT29/Cozy Bear and to Russia's Africa influence operation — the SVR absorbed Wagner's influence/disinfo arm ("The Company," ~100 consultants). The quieter, foreign-facing, influence-oriented leg of the competing triad (composition guard).
persons.drivers: Foreign-intelligence advantage; strategic influence abroad (notably Africa/Sahel alignment); institutional and personal standing in the vertical.
persons.worldview: Interpretation: a foreign-policy-adjacent, influence-and-espionage view of statecraft; publicly articulates Kremlin narratives, positioning Russia against Western hegemony.
persons.decision_style: Interpretation: more political and public-facing than the GRU chief, oriented to influence operations and long-horizon espionage rather than overt sabotage. Personal detail thin.
persons.track_record: SVR director since 2016; the service is linked to APT29 (Cozy Bear) — a stealthy, patient cyber-espionage actor — and to the post-Wagner Africa influence apparatus. Personal operational decisions largely opaque.

persons.name: William Hinman

Ripple. A documented appearance-of-conflict — continued multi-million-dollar payments from his former firm while at the SEC — was flagged by the SEC IG, but actual corruption is not asserted.
persons.drivers: Public role: providing regulatory guidance on token classification. The documented financial fact — continued payments from Simpson Thacher (an Enterprise Ethereum Alliance member) during his SEC tenure — is noted as an appearance-of-conflict question, not proven motive.
persons.worldview: Interpretation (Moderate): a decentralization-based test for when a token ceases to be a security — pragmatic and asset-specific rather than categorical. Drawn from the speech's own logic.

persons.track_record: The 14 Jun 2018 speech (BTC & ETH not securities); the 'Hinman documents' ordered unsealed and made central to SEC v. Ripple on selective-treatment/fair-notice grounds. The speech, the payments, and the IG concern are all fact; corruption is not asserted.
persons.vulnerabilities: The appearance-of-conflict is the core exposure: SEC IG and Empower Oversight (via FOIA) flagged the Simpson Thacher payments while Hinman shaped ETH policy. It is an ethics question, explicitly graded as not proven corruption.

persons.legitimacy_bearing: The documented appearance-of-conflict at the center of a market-defining regulatory decision is itself a legitimacy question about whether crypto policy was made at arm's length — regardless of whether any wrongdoing occurred.
persons.confidence: Moderate-to-High on facts (speech, IG concern, payments all documented); Low on any inference of intent, which the corpus explicitly declines to assert.
persons.grade: Solid: the facts are well-sourced; the analysis carefully separates documented conflict-appearance from unproven corruption.

persons.name: Joseph Van Loon

persons.bluf: Van Loon is the lead plaintiff in the successful challenge to OFAC's Tornado Cash sanctions: on 26 Nov 2024 a unanimous 5th Circuit panel held the immutable smart contracts are not 'property' under IEEPA ('just software code,' no owner), finding OFAC overstepped; Treasury delisted Tornado Cash on 21 Mar 2025.
persons.drivers: Public record: as a plaintiff, challenging the lawfulness of sanctioning immutable, ownerless smart-contract code. Specific personal motives beyond the litigation posture are not detailed in the corpus.
persons.worldview: Interpretation (Moderate): a view that immutable open-source protocols are not sanctionable property and that OFAC's designation exceeded its statutory authority — the position the 5th Circuit ultimately credited. Inferred from the suit; labeled interpretation.

persons.track_record: Lead plaintiff in Van Loon v. Treasury; the 5th Circuit ruled unanimously (26 Nov 2024) that the immutable contracts are not 'property' under IEEPA and that OFAC overstepped; Treasury delisted Tornado Cash (21 Mar 2025). A clear litigation win on the sanctions question. Fact.
persons.vulnerabilities: As a plaintiff his exposure is limited; the notable tension is that the successful sanctions challenge coexists with the separate, ongoing criminal developer-liability prosecutions (Storm/Pertsev) — the law and the courts moved in different directions on adjacent questions.

persons.outlook: Interpretation: the Van Loon ruling and the March 2025 delisting are among the clearest legal rebukes of the immutable-code sanctions theory, feeding the 2025 reversal — a durable precedent on the property question.
persons.legitimacy_bearing: As a plaintiff who prevailed, his suit bears on legitimacy by demonstrating judicial check on agency overreach — the courts constraining OFAC where it exceeded IEEPA. Described neutrally as successful litigation.

persons.grade: Solid on the case and outcome; short on personal specifics, kept proportionate.

persons.legitimacy_bearing: His case bears on legitimacy at the state level — a government publicly honoring a U.S.-sanctioned crime figure signals apparent state complicity, which the workers (forced-labor/trafficking victims) and defrauded victims pay for. Individual conduct is not equated with the Laotian or Chinese state.

persons.grade: Solid on sanction/enterprise/medal facts; interpretive on state-complicity, clearly flagged; composition guard applied.

persons.name: Anthony Albanese
persons.role: Prime Minister of Australia (Australian Labor Party); leader of the Labor government since 2022
persons.bluf: Albanese heads a US-aligned economy structurally dependent on Chinese demand — the central bind of the Australia state-network file. Under him Australia simultaneously deepened AUKUS security integration, signed the US-Australia critical-minerals framework (Oct 2025, with Trump), and remained export-dependent on the very customer (China) it is arming against. He runs a hedging/split-dependency posture: security with one bloc, trade with its rival.
persons.drivers: Interpretation: keeping Australia inside the US/UK security umbrella while preserving the China trade that funds the economy; delivering domestic economic security (critical-minerals reserve, ~A$4T super pool policy, the 12% Superannuation Guarantee, Division 296). Grounded driver = managing split dependency, not ideology.
persons.worldview: Interpretation: alliance-first on security, pragmatic-transactional on trade; treats the US relationship as the anchor even under a transactional Trump administration (Jun 2025 DoD AUKUS review, Oct 2025 Trump signal of support). Reads Australia's future as a Western critical-minerals alternative to Chinese dominance.
persons.decision_style: Interpretation from record: incremental, coalition-managing, risk-averse; accepted a revised AUKUS submarine pathway (three second-hand Virginia-class boats plus SSN-AUKUS; A$310M paid to UK Feb 2026) rather than forcing terms. Manages exposure rather than resolving the contradiction.
persons.track_record: US-Australia critical-minerals framework (Oct 2025); A$1.2B Critical Minerals Strategic Reserve; A$940M PsiQuantum Brisbane commitment; 12% super from Jul 2025. On the record for keeping AUKUS alive through the US review and a re-elected Labor government.
persons.vulnerabilities: China's weaponized buyer power (centralized iron-ore purchasing via China Mineral Resources Group; BHP Jinbao brand restrictions, ~$2.8B/yr hit) directly threatens Australia's largest export; AUKUS execution risk and dependence on US/UK industrial capacity; the structural inconsistency itself (arming against the dominant customer).
persons.relationships: US (Trump administration; AUKUS, minerals framework) and UK (SSN-AUKUS); China as dominant trade counterpart wielding monopsony power; domestic mining majors (BHP, Rio Tinto, Fortescue) and Lynas (first non-China commercial dysprosium oxide, May 2025).
persons.outlook: Interpretation: the split-dependency posture likely persists — Australia continues to hedge, deepening US/UK security ties and the minerals pivot while absorbing Chinese trade pressure. The unresolved contradiction is the durable risk.
persons.legitimacy_bearing: Holds democratic-electoral legitimacy as elected head of government of a parliamentary democracy. Domestic mandate is genuine; the file's critique is of Australia's institutional posture, not his authority to govern.
persons.confidence: Moderate — Australia's institutional actions (AUKUS terms, minerals framework, mining/China figures, super rules) are well-documented fact per the file; his personal drivers/worldview are labeled interpretation.
persons.grade: Solid on the structural facts and Albanese's public role; the 'caught between US security and China trade' read is graded/interpretive per the source file, and behavioral notes are inference not diagnosis.

persons.name: Shinzo Abe
persons.role: Former Prime Minister of Japan (LDP); Japan's longest-serving PM; architect of 'Abenomics'; assassinated 8 July 2022 (deceased) — a historical dossier
persons.bluf: Abe (deceased) is the historical hinge of the Japan politics file: his assassination on 8 July 2022 by Tetsuya Yamagami — motivated by grievance against the South-Korea-based Unification Church that had bankrupted the killer's family via his mother's ~$700k+ in donations — exposed the decades-long Church-LDP nexus (an LDP internal survey later found nearly half its lawmakers had church ties; the Tokyo District Court ordered the church dissolved, Mar 2025). Abe's grandfather, PM Kishi, had helped bring the church to Japan.
persons.drivers: Interpretation (historical): LDP dominance, a more assertive/'normal-nation' security posture, and economic revival via Abenomics (monetary easing, fiscal stimulus, structural reform). Grounded facts = his role as longest-serving PM and Abenomics; internal motives are inference.
persons.worldview: Interpretation: nationalist-conservative, alliance-oriented (US-Japan), favoring a stronger Japanese defense/international role and reflationary economics. Well-established public record, framed here as interpretation of a deceased figure.
persons.decision_style: Interpretation: consolidator of LDP power and long-horizon strategist (the durability of his tenure and the Abenomics program). Not analyzed in the source file beyond the assassination context; treat as background inference.
persons.track_record: Longest-serving PM of Japan; 'Abenomics' is his signature economic program. The file's specific, documented thread is the assassination and its exposure of the Church-LDP relationship (his family's historical tie via Kishi; the killer's church grievance).
persons.vulnerabilities: Historical/legacy: the exposed Church-LDP association became a liability for the party after his death; his security was fatally breached in the 2022 attack. (These are legacy vulnerabilities, not those of a sitting leader.)
persons.relationships: LDP (which he long led); the Unification Church network (via family history and the party's ground game); grandfather PM Kishi as the historical origin of the church tie; US-Japan alliance as his foreign-policy anchor.
persons.outlook: Not applicable — deceased. Legacy: the post-assassination fallout reshaped LDP-church scrutiny and fed the file's fragmentation/foreign-influence themes; the church was ordered dissolved (Mar 2025).
persons.legitimacy_bearing: Held democratic-electoral legitimacy as an elected PM. The file's concern is the party's foreign-linked influence exposure surfaced by his killing, not the legitimacy of his office.
persons.confidence: Moderate — the assassination, the killer's motive, the Church-LDP survey, the Kishi tie, and the court dissolution are documented fact in the file; Abenomics and his tenure are well-established public record; personal worldview/decision-style are interpretation.
persons.grade: Solid on the historical facts grounded in the file and public record; clearly framed as a former/deceased leader; behavioral notes are interpretation.

persons.name: Shigeru Ishiba
persons.role: Prime Minister of Japan (LDP), governing as a weakened minority after the LDP lost its majority in both Diet chambers

His premiership sits atop the file's stress stack: fragmentation, the Sanseito 'Japanese First' anti-foreigner surge (2 -> 15 upper-house seats), a demographic crunch, and youth in crisis.
persons.drivers: Interpretation: holding the LDP-led government together and governing through gridlock without a majority; managing the anti-foreigner/populist pressure and the demographic-economic strain. Grounded fact = the minority position; specific personal drivers are inference.

Treat worldview specifics as inference beyond the file.
persons.decision_style: Interpretation: constrained, negotiation-dependent governance forced by minority status — must bargain with opposition (CDP largest; DPP/Ishin gained) to pass anything. The file establishes the structural constraint, not a documented personal style.
persons.track_record: Became PM and then presided over the historic double loss of Diet majorities (Oct 2024 lower house; Jul 2025 upper house, coalition winning just 47 of 125 contested seats). Governs amid the Sanseito surge and the demographic/youth-crisis backdrop the file details.
persons.vulnerabilities: No majority in either chamber (structural weakness/gridlock); the rising Sanseito populist-right challenge; the demographic crunch (2025 births ~706,000; ~11:1 elderly-to-children social-security spend) and youth-crisis pressures constraining any government; LDP's lingering Unification-Church association.

persons.outlook: Interpretation: continued precarious minority governance and coalition/opposition bargaining; elevated risk from populist-right momentum and the unrelenting demographic strain. Instability is the base case per the file's fragmentation frame.
persons.legitimacy_bearing: Holds democratic-electoral legitimacy as PM but with a visibly weakened parliamentary mandate (no majority in either chamber), which materially limits his governing authority.
persons.confidence: Moderate — the electoral results, his minority status, the Sanseito surge, and the demographic figures are documented fact in the file; his personal worldview/decision-style are interpretation.
persons.grade: Solid on the structural/electoral facts grounded in the file; personal drivers and style are interpretation, and party-vs-individual motive claims are explicitly avoided.

persons.name: Kenneth Feinberg
persons.role: Mass-tort "special master" and claims-fund administrator — the recurring neutral who designs and runs large compensation systems (9/11 Victim Compensation Fund, BP Deepwater Horizon Gulf Coast Claims Facility, and other high-profile funds)
persons.bluf: Feinberg is the archetype of the mass-tort settlement economy's administrative layer: when catastrophic liability is converted into a pooled compensation fund rather than litigated case-by-case, he is the figure repeatedly appointed to design the matrix, set award values, and adjudicate claims. He sits at the exact seam the corpus flags — where adjudicated harm becomes a long-dated, administrator-run payout stream.
persons.drivers: Interpretation: a self-styled belief in efficient, out-of-court compensation as more humane and faster than adversarial litigation; professional identity as a trusted neutral; reputational capital as the go-to "special master." (Reading of his public role, not a documented statement of motive.)
persons.worldview: Interpretation: pragmatic and utilitarian — that discretionary, formula-driven compensation delivers more to more victims sooner than the tort lottery, at the cost of individualized justice and transparency. Believes a single credible neutral can substitute for years of litigation.
persons.decision_style: Interpretation: centralized and discretionary; builds explicit award matrices and valuation formulas, then exercises wide personal judgment on individual awards. Values speed, finality, and being seen as fair by claimants.
persons.track_record: Publicly established as administrator/special master of the 9/11 VCF and the BP Gulf Coast Claims Facility, among other funds; widely regarded as the defining practitioner of the role. This dossier does not assert specific dollar totals or dates for those funds beyond what is publicly established, to avoid fabricating figures.
persons.vulnerabilities: Interpretation: the neutrality of a paid administrator is inherently contestable — who pays him, how award matrices are set, and the opacity of discretionary awards draw recurring criticism. The settlement-fund model itself (money over decades, finality traded for individualized justice) is the structure the corpus critiques.
persons.relationships: Structurally adjacent to every actor in the mass-tort lineage the corpus maps — the §524(g) asbestos trust template (spec-asbestos-mass-tort), the opioid settlement streams (spec-opioid-crisis), and the tobacco-MSA payout model. Not tied in the source files to any specific defendant; functions as the neutral in the middle.
persons.outlook: Interpretation: as long as mass-harm liability keeps resolving into administered funds rather than trials, the special-master/claims-administrator function he defined remains structurally central, whoever holds the pen.
persons.legitimacy_bearing: Legitimacy rests on perceived neutrality and court appointment, not on ownership or capital. His authority is delegated and reputational — it survives only as long as claimants and courts trust the fairness of a discretionary administrator.
persons.confidence: Moderate — his role and prominence are well-established public record, but the provided research files cover the mass-tort structure (asbestos §524(g), opioids) rather than Feinberg personally, so specifics beyond his public reputation are deliberately not asserted.
persons.grade: Solid on role and structural placement; thin on individual specifics (no file-grounded amounts/dates for his funds). Behavioral notes are interpretation of the public record, not documented motive.

persons.name: Sackler Family
persons.role: Owning family of Purdue Pharma, maker of OxyContin — a family/collective actor at the origin of the prescription-opioid wave and the central defendant in the opioid settlement economy
persons.bluf: As a collective, the Sackler family owned and directed the manufacturer that marketed OxyContin with downplayed addiction risk from 1996, extracted roughly $11B before Purdue's bankruptcy, and attempted to cap their personal liability via non-consensual third-party releases — a tactic the Supreme Court struck down in Harrington v. Purdue (June 2024). This is the canonical institutional-harm-to-settlement pipeline.
persons.drivers: Interpretation (collective, not imputed to every member): preservation of family wealth and reputation; use of corporate and bankruptcy structures to contain liability. Described as a family/collective actor — individual members held differing roles and knowledge, and no single mind should be imputed to all.
persons.worldview: Interpretation: treated legal and bankruptcy machinery as instruments for insulating owners from the consequences of the operating company's conduct — the "bankrupt-a-subsidiary-to-release-the-owners" logic. (Read of the collective's litigation posture, not a clinical or unanimous-intent claim.)
persons.decision_style: Interpretation: worked through the corporate entity and sophisticated legal/financial advisors; extracted wealth ahead of the liability wave, then sought comprehensive releases in exchange for a capped, staged payout.
persons.track_record: Fact (per spec-opioid-crisis): Purdue marketed OxyContin with downplayed addiction risk from 1996; the family extracted ~$11B; used bankruptcy to seek non-consensual third-party releases; SCOTUS struck those releases (June 2024, Harrington v. Purdue); renegotiated deal ~$7.4B (Jan 2025), plan approved 18 Nov 2025 — Sacklers to pay ~$1.5B + Purdue ~$900M first tranche (early 2026), 15-year state payout.
persons.vulnerabilities: Interpretation grounded in the record: the SCOTUS ruling removed their preferred liability shield; reputational damage is severe and durable; the settlement converts real adjudicated culpability into a decades-long payout that arrives after the harm. Future exposure depends on the finality the approved plan actually delivers.
persons.relationships: Central to the opioid cluster: parallels the J&J Texas two-step (same release logic SCOTUS constrained); connected structurally to the Big Three distributors + J&J (~$26B) and pharmacy settlements in the same crisis, and to the broader §524(g)/tobacco-MSA settlement-economy template.
persons.outlook: Interpretation: financial exposure is now largely fixed by the approved ~$7.4B plan, but the family's tactic (owner releases via subsidiary bankruptcy) is precedentially weakened for future mass torts; reputational liability is permanent.
persons.legitimacy_bearing: Legitimacy is essentially forfeit — as a collective the family carries adjudicated and settled culpability for mass harm. Their remaining standing is contractual (the settlement) rather than moral or civic.
persons.confidence: High — the core facts (marketing, ~$11B extraction, the SCOTUS ruling, the ~$7.4B redo and its schedule) are directly grounded in spec-opioid-crisis and the well-established record.
persons.grade: Solid on facts and structure. Treated as a family/collective actor per the composition guard; motive statements are labeled interpretation and not imputed uniformly to individual members.

persons.name: Louis Rossmann

His public rebuttals to the auto industry's "cybersecurity/safety" framing align with the dated record (NHTSA's own walk-back; the MA law upheld Feb 2025).

persons.decision_style: Interpretation: public-facing and adversarial — long-form video advocacy, direct rebuttal of industry framing, and institution-building (FULU 501(c)(4), a DRM-circumvention bounty program, the Consumer Rights Wiki) rather than inside lobbying.
persons.track_record: Fact (per spec-right-to-repair, spec-auto-right-to-repair): long-time repair advocate; was based at/funded by FUTO (Eron Wolf's grants program), left FUTO in 2025 to co-found the FULU Foundation (501(c)(4)) with Kevin O'Reilly and launch the Consumer Rights Wiki (Jan 2025); publicly rebutted the auto industry's "weaponized car" strawman, a critique that aligns with NHTSA's reversal and the court upholding the MA telematics law (Feb 2025).
persons.vulnerabilities: Interpretation: an advocate, not a neutral — his framing is documented advocacy, not adopted as fact by the corpus. Note the corpus's explicit correction: the common phrasing "Rossmann funds FUTO" is inverted — FUTO funded the work; he later moved to FULU. His platform reach depends on channels the corpus can't independently fetch/quote.

persons.outlook: Interpretation: aligned with a genuinely advancing legal tide (50-state bills, ~6 enacted electronics laws, parts-pairing bans, EU Repair Directive, §1201 exemptions, the REPAIR Act) even as specific vehicles stall (R2R stripped from the 2026 NDAA). His structural position strengthens as the dated record cuts toward repair rights.

persons.confidence: Moderate-to-High — his role, the FUTO→FULU move, the Consumer Rights Wiki, and his auto-industry rebuttal are directly grounded in the two R2R files. Behavioral notes are interpretation of that public advocacy.

persons.drivers: Interpretation: growth and equity value in a capital-intensive, credit-dependent business; extension of a decades-long family franchise in subprime auto (DriveTime → Carvana) that monetizes access-to-credit for thin-file borrowers.
persons.worldview: Interpretation: subprime auto as a scalable, securitizable lending business where volume and the ABS forward-flow, not underwriting caution, drive returns — with related-party servicing (DriveTime) as a structural backstop.
persons.decision_style: Interpretation: aggressive, leverage-tolerant, founder/family-controlled — Carvana nearly collapsed under debt in 2022 then sharply recovered (2023-25); decision-making concentrated in the family across the retail and lending sides.
persons.track_record: Fact (per spec-auto-credit-unwind): Ernest Garcia III is Carvana CEO; Ernest Garcia II is Carvana's largest shareholder and owns DriveTime, which backstops Carvana's loans; Garcia II pleaded guilty to bank fraud in the 1990 Lincoln Savings/Keating S&L scandal; Carvana nearly collapsed in 2022 and recovered 2023-25. Carvana runs on subprime lending + securitization (forward-flow to Ally + auto ABS).
persons.vulnerabilities: Interpretation grounded in the record: related-party (DriveTime) conflicts and loan-quality opacity; exposure to record subprime-auto 60+ day delinquencies, negative equity, and rising repossessions (~$1.6T auto balances); reputational overhang from Garcia II's prior fraud conviction; sensitivity to the 2025 auto-credit unwind mood (Dimon's "cockroaches"). Note: no fraud is alleged against Carvana/Garcia in the source — unlike Tricolor/First Brands, whose fraud claims are flagged as alleged/under-investigation.
persons.relationships: Sits in the auto-credit-unwind cluster alongside the (fraud-alleged) Tricolor and First Brands failures and their lenders (Jefferies, UBS, JPMorgan, Fifth Third); tied to Ally via loan forward-flow; DriveTime↔Carvana is the key related-party link. Family control (father/son) is the defining internal relationship.
persons.outlook: Interpretation: the complex survived 2022 and recovered, but remains structurally exposed to the subprime-auto credit cycle and to contagion sentiment from the 2025 unwind; the systemic-vs-contained reading is presented both ways in the source and is not resolved.
persons.legitimacy_bearing: Legitimacy is market/ownership-based (control via shareholding and a going concern), carrying a reputational discount from Garcia II's prior bank-fraud conviction and from the related-party structure — but no adjudicated wrongdoing at Carvana itself in the record.
persons.confidence: Moderate-to-High — the family control structure, the DriveTime backstop, the S&L guilty plea, and the macro backdrop are directly grounded in spec-auto-credit-unwind; forward-looking and motive statements are labeled interpretation.
persons.grade: Solid on structure and the documented facts; careful not to impute fraud to Carvana/Garcia (none alleged in the source); drivers/worldview labeled interpretation.

persons.name: Neville Roy Singham
persons.role: Tech millionaire (sold Thoughtworks, ~$785M); self-described Marxist based in Shanghai; funder of a nonprofit network that a Bitcoin Policy Institute series alleges backs a China-aligned campaign against US AI data centers — an allegation presented here with its grade, not as established fact
persons.bluf: There is a documented, CCP-aligned network around Singham (NYT 2023; IRS 990s reading ~$278M into six US nonprofits 2017-23; a Delhi Police chargesheet naming the Chinese government the network's "ultimate paymaster" in 2024; a Rubio FARA referral and reported DOJ grand-jury probe). Activists from that ecosystem genuinely joined ~21 US data-center fights. But the BPI thesis that this constitutes a directed foreign-influence campaign that blocked "$23.6B" is a CONTESTED, graded claim — the reports themselves hedge causation, the dollar figure has no stated methodology, the publisher is a pro-AI/pro-Bitcoin institute, and Singham's network denies direction.
persons.drivers: Interpretation: stated ideological conviction (self-described Marxist) funding anti-imperialist/progressive and China-aligned advocacy. His own stated position (2023): he is "not a member of, work for, take orders from, or follow instructions of any political party or government." That denial is on the record and must be carried.
persons.worldview: Interpretation: an anti-imperialist, Global-South-oriented, explicitly Marxist frame; his funded outlets echo Chinese-government positions (e.g., Tricontinental opposing US chip export controls). Whether this reflects independent ideological alignment or direction is exactly the disputed question.
persons.decision_style: Interpretation: funds through an opaque network of nonprofits (some, like PSL, exempt from public financial reporting); conspicuously does not disclose the financing — which the corpus reads (via read-words-for-intent) as cutting toward alignment, while stressing that non-disclosure + alignment is NOT command.
persons.track_record: Fact (per spec-ai-datacenter-foreign-influence, influence-operator-network): sold Thoughtworks ~$785M; prior Huawei consulting; per NYT (2023) worked from a Shanghai office shared with a Chinese media firm and attended a CCP propaganda workshop; ~$278M traced (Fox reading of 990s) into The People's Forum, ANSWER, BreakThrough News, Tricontinental, Justice and Education Fund, CodePink (co-founded by his spouse Jodie Evans); PSL-overlapping activists took part in ~21 data-center fights in 14 states. ALLEGED/GRADED: that this directed the opposition, and the $23.6B blocked figure (weak — no methodology).
persons.vulnerabilities: Interpretation grounded in the record: active legal/regulatory exposure — Rubio FARA referral (2023), reported DOJ grand-jury probe, the Delhi "ultimate paymaster" chargesheet (May 2024), and Congressional inquiries. Reputationally exposed to the "CCP influence" framing even where the strongest version is unproven.
persons.relationships: Spouse Jodie Evans (CodePink co-founder); funded-network leadership overlaps the Party for Socialism and Liberation (De la Cruz, Garcia, the Beckers, Puryear, De Los Santos, Benjamin, La Riva). In the corpus he cross-links to CCP, Huawei, US Congress, AI Datacenters, and the Bitcoin Policy Institute (the framing publisher, whose pro-AI interest is itself flagged).
persons.outlook: Interpretation: continued legal/political pressure (FARA/DOJ) is likely regardless of whether the maximalist "directed campaign" claim is ever substantiated; the documented network + genuine participation are durable facts, while the causation and aggregate-impact claims remain contested and, per the composition guard, must not be inflated into "the movement is a Chinese operation."
persons.legitimacy_bearing: Legitimacy is contested and low in the US policy arena given the FARA referral and probe; he presents as a private ideological funder exercising protected speech/association, while critics frame the network as undisclosed foreign influence. The corpus treats the network as real and the "direction" claim as graded/allegation.
persons.confidence: Moderate — the network facts are High-confidence and well-corroborated (NYT, 990s, Delhi chargesheet, FARA referral); the "directed foreign-influence campaign" and the $23.6B figure are explicitly Low/weak and contested, and are presented as allegation with grade, per instruction.
persons.grade: Split by claim: network existence + participation = solid/fact; foreign-DIRECTION of the anti-data-center movement = graded inference the reports themselves hedge; $23.6B aggregate = unsubstantiated (no methodology). Denials carried; composition/causation guard applied.

persons.name: Julie Inman Grant
persons.role: Australia's eSafety Commissioner — the independent online-safety regulator whose office administers the framework behind the world-first under-16 social-media ban (effective 10 Dec 2025) that Meta and other platforms had to implement
persons.bluf: As eSafety Commissioner, Inman Grant heads the statutory Australian regulator behind the under-16 platform ban that took effect 10 Dec 2025 — the measure Meta responded to (blocking under-16s from 4 Dec, ~550k accounts removed within a day; ~4.7M industry-wide). Her office also runs the post-implementation enforcement review that surfaced the compliance gap (~7 in 10 surveyed parents said their child still had an account).
persons.drivers: Interpretation: a regulator's mandate to reduce online harms to minors; institutional interest in demonstrating that the world-first ban is enforceable and effective. (Read of the office's public posture, described neutrally.)
persons.worldview: Interpretation: that platform design and age-based access controls are legitimate, enforceable subjects of state regulation, and that platforms bear obligations to keep minors off their services — an activist-regulator stance on online harms.
persons.decision_style: Interpretation: statutory and enforcement-driven — sets codes/obligations, monitors platform compliance, publishes findings on gaps, and holds platforms accountable through the regulator's powers rather than through litigation.
persons.track_record: Fact (per influence-meta-childsafety): Australia's world-first under-16 ban took effect 10 Dec 2025; Meta began blocking under-16s 4 Dec and removed ~550k accounts within a day (~4.7M industry-wide); eSafety's later review found ~7 in 10 surveyed parents reported their child still had an account (an enforcement gap). The file references the eSafety Commissioner's regulatory role in this record; it does not assert biographical specifics, which are therefore not fabricated here.
persons.vulnerabilities: Interpretation grounded in the record: the ban's credibility depends on closing the documented enforcement gap; platforms (notably Meta) publicly argue age-verification responsibility should sit with app stores rather than the platforms/regulator — a liability-shift contest the regulator is caught in. Effectiveness questions, not misconduct, are the exposure.
persons.relationships: Regulatory counterparty to Meta and other major platforms; her framework parallels the EU DSA minors proceedings and the UK OSA/Ofcom Protection of Children Codes as the international online-harms regulatory wave. In the corpus she is the state/regulator node the Meta child-safety thread responds to.
persons.outlook: Interpretation: near-term focus is enforcement and closing the parent-reported compliance gap; the Australian model is a template other jurisdictions watch, so her office's success or failure at making the ban real has influence beyond Australia.
persons.legitimacy_bearing: Legitimacy is statutory and office-based — she acts as an independent public regulator under Australian law, with authority derived from mandate and process rather than capital or ownership. Described neutrally: a regulator exercising delegated public power.
persons.confidence: Moderate — her regulatory role and the under-16 ban record (dates, Meta's response, the enforcement-gap finding) are grounded in influence-meta-childsafety; specific biographical detail is not in the source and is deliberately not invented. Behavioral notes are interpretation.
persons.grade: Solid on role and the ban/enforcement record; neutral framing per the regulator instruction; drivers/worldview labeled interpretation, biography left unasserted where the source is silent.

persons.name: Chris Larsen
persons.bluf: Larsen co-founded Ripple (fka OpenCoin, 2012) with Jed McCaleb and Arthur Britto and remains its executive chairman; the firm controls a large share of the 100B XRP premine via on-ledger escrow. He steered Ripple through the SEC case (final $125M penalty, 2023 split ruling) into an institutional-rails build-out (Hidden Road/Ripple Prime, RLUSD). His fortune and influence are tied to XRP's value and Ripple's TradFi positioning.
persons.drivers: Building payment rails that displace/augment correspondent banking; monetizing the XRP premine; legitimizing Ripple as institutional infrastructure. A serial fintech founder (prior: E-Loan, Prosper) with a long-running interest in disintermediating incumbent finance.
persons.worldview: Fintech-reformist rather than crypto-anarchist: works with regulators and banks, markets XRP as a compliant bridge asset technically aligned with ISO 20022, and positions Ripple inside institutional finance rather than against it.
persons.decision_style: Institutional and litigation-tolerant: absorbed a multi-year SEC fight, pursued acquisitions (Hidden Road $1.25B, Rail, GTreasury) to vertically integrate, and manages the premine via structured escrow to avoid crashing the market. Politically active (crypto-industry donor/advocacy).
persons.track_record: Co-founded Ripple; oversaw creation of XRPL, Ripple Payments/ODL, and RLUSD (NYDFS-approved Dec 2024); navigated SEC v. Ripple to a partial win (programmatic sales not securities) with a $125M penalty; built Ripple Prime out of Hidden Road.
persons.vulnerabilities: Concentrated wealth in XRP and Ripple equity; the $125M penalty and 2023 institutional-sales finding stand as adverse precedent; heavy reliance on XRP escrow means his position is exposed to XRP price and to the gap between real TradFi alignment and the over-claimed 'banks-run-on-XRP' lore.
persons.relationships: Co-founders Jed McCaleb (departed, now Stellar) and Arthur Britto; Ripple leadership David Schwartz (CTO), Brad Garlinghouse (CEO), Stuart Alderoty (legal). Regulatory adversary: the SEC (Clayton-era filing, Gensler-era litigation).
persons.outlook: Watch: Ripple Prime institutional traction and RLUSD adoption; whether XRPL settlement actually lands at named institutions vs. remaining 'alignment'; further acquisitions; XRP escrow-release cadence; any renewed regulatory posture shift.
persons.confidence: High — the Ripple/SEC/acquisition record is documented and dated in the corpus.
persons.grade: Solid on documented corporate/legal facts; motivations and worldview are reasoned interpretation of the public record, not asserted intent.

persons.name: Arthur Britto
persons.bluf: Britto is one of the three original architects of the XRP Ledger (2011-12) alongside David Schwartz and Jed McCaleb, and a Ripple co-founder, but he remained deliberately low-profile/anonymous for most of the project's history. He is a foundational technical figure whose public footprint is thin by design. Assessment is necessarily limited.
persons.drivers: Building the XRPL as a technical artifact; apparent preference for privacy over public prominence. Specific personal motivations are not documented in the corpus.
persons.worldview: Not documented in the research files beyond his role as a builder of a permissionless-settlement ledger; any ideological read would be speculation.
persons.decision_style: Chose sustained anonymity/low visibility — itself a risk-and-exposure decision — but his internal decision process is not evidenced.
persons.track_record: Co-created the XRP Ledger and co-founded Ripple; long associated with the early premine/allocation. Later public/associated with XRPL-adjacent ventures, though details are sparse in the corpus.
persons.vulnerabilities: As an XRP premine insider his exposure tracks XRP and any legacy allocation, but the corpus does not document specifics; thin public record limits any lever assessment.
persons.relationships: Co-founders Chris Larsen, Jed McCaleb, and David Schwartz (fellow XRPL creator).
persons.outlook: Watch: any emergence into public commentary; disclosures about early XRP allocations tied to founders.
persons.confidence: Low — deliberately anonymous; the corpus documents his co-creator role but little else.
persons.grade: Thin. Grounded only on the co-creator/co-founder fact; everything beyond that is under-documented and left short by design.

persons.name: Jed McCaleb
persons.role: Ripple co-founder; founder of Stellar (SDF); ex-Mt. Gox
persons.bluf: McCaleb is a serial crypto founder whose arc links Mt. Gox (created 2010, sold 2011), Ripple (co-founder/CTO until ~2013), and Stellar (SDF, 2014, with Joyce Kim). His ~9B XRP settlement and the on-ledger 'tacostand' sell-down — tracked publicly until emptied July 2022 — make him the cleanest on-chain-verifiable insider case in the corpus. He is a builder who repeatedly leaves over decentralization/governance disputes.
persons.drivers: Building decentralized payment networks; a stated preference for more-decentralized, nonprofit-governed designs (the reason he left Ripple). Also managing an orderly, market-sensitive exit from a very large XRP position.
persons.worldview: More decentralization-idealist than Larsen: left Ripple over its direction/centralization and founded a nonprofit (SDF) with a rewritten consensus (SCP, 2015), positioning Stellar as a technically distinct, mission-driven cousin of XRPL.
persons.decision_style: Iterative and exit-prone: founds, disputes direction, departs, rebuilds. Accepted a 2014 settlement capping daily XRP sales (tied to trading volume) and a pledge to donate 2B XRP — a negotiated, transparent wind-down rather than a dump.
persons.track_record: Created Mt. Gox and eDonkey; co-founded Ripple; co-founded Stellar and co-authored SCP; drove Stellar's real TradFi ties (Circle/USDC, DTCC work, Franklin Templeton BENJI, MoneyGram/IBM World Wire). Completed the tacostand sell-down (Jul 2022).
persons.vulnerabilities: Legacy Mt. Gox association (though he sold before the 2014 collapse); large historical XRP/XLM holdings; Stellar's token-value-accrual, like most L1s, is debated even where pilots are real.
persons.relationships: Stellar co-founder Joyce Kim; former Ripple co-founders Larsen and Britto (split); Mt. Gox successor Mark Karpelès; partners Circle, DTCC, Franklin Templeton, MoneyGram.
persons.outlook: Watch: SDF governance and Stellar's TradFi tokenization pipeline; McCaleb's other ventures (e.g., aerospace) drawing attention/capital away; residual XRP/XLM holdings.
persons.confidence: High — his lineage, the 2014 settlement, and the tacostand emptying are on-chain/documented facts.
persons.grade: Strong. Built on Wikipedia, Ripple/SDF posts, on-ledger data, and partner PRs; decentralization-idealist read is labeled interpretation.

persons.name: Joyce Kim
persons.bluf: Kim co-founded the nonprofit Stellar Development Foundation with Jed McCaleb in 2014 and served as an early executive director before moving into venture investing. She is documented in the corpus primarily as SDF co-founder; her deeper strategic profile is thinly sourced here. Assessment is kept correspondingly short.
persons.drivers: Building a nonprofit-governed financial-inclusion network (SDF's stated mission); later, backing early-stage founders as an investor. Specifics of personal motivation are not detailed in the corpus.
persons.worldview: Aligned with Stellar's financial-inclusion / more-decentralized, nonprofit framing versus for-profit Ripple; a former attorney with a startup/VC orientation.
persons.decision_style: Not documented in the research files; her role in SDF's early strategy and her later shift to investing suggest an institution-building then capital-allocation path, but decision specifics are not evidenced here.
persons.track_record: Co-founded SDF and helped launch Stellar in its early years; subsequently active in venture capital. The corpus documents the co-founding fact and SDF's institutional ties.
persons.vulnerabilities: Thin public documentation in the corpus limits any lever assessment; no legal or financial exposure is documented here.
persons.relationships: Co-founder Jed McCaleb; the Stellar Development Foundation and its partner ecosystem (Circle, MoneyGram, Franklin Templeton).
persons.outlook: Watch: her venture investments and any continued involvement in the Stellar/crypto ecosystem.
persons.confidence: Low — corpus documents the co-founder role but little strategic detail.
persons.grade: Thin. Grounded on the SDF co-founding fact from the Stellar block; broader profile under-documented and intentionally kept brief.

persons.name: Evan Cheng
persons.role: Mysten Labs CEO (Sui blockchain); ex-Meta Novi, LLVM contributor
persons.bluf: Cheng is CEO of Mysten Labs, the ex-Meta Diem/Novi 'diaspora' shop that built the Move-language L1 Sui. Mysten raised ~$300M at a >$2B valuation led by FTX Ventures (later disgraced in the FTX collapse) with a16z. His profile is the corpus's worked example that elite pedigree plus big capital is not validation.
persons.drivers: Building high-performance blockchain infrastructure (Sui + ecosystem like Walrus storage); commercializing a compiler/systems background into a well-capitalized L1. A veteran engineer (LLVM, Apple, Meta) turned crypto-company CEO.
persons.worldview: Big-Tech systems-engineering pragmatism carried into crypto: performance and developer-experience framing (Move, object model) over ideological decentralization rhetoric.
persons.decision_style: Well-capitalized, pedigree-forward institution building; accepted FTX Ventures money at a high valuation — a bet that later looked less like validation than the corpus's cautionary point.
persons.track_record: Led LLVM/compiler work and engineering at Apple and Meta; co-founded Mysten Labs and shipped Sui and its ecosystem (Walrus, ~$140M token sale). Part of the ex-Diem cohort that also produced Aptos.
persons.vulnerabilities: Reputational tie to FTX Ventures as a lead backer; L1 token value-accrual is debated (altcoin-lens: real pilots, weak accrual); competitive crowding among ex-Diem chains; 'pedigree ≠ validation' framing sits on the record.
persons.relationships: Co-founder Sam Blackshear (Move creator); backers FTX Ventures (disgraced) and a16z; the broader Diem/Novi diaspora (incl. Aptos).
persons.outlook: Watch: Sui adoption vs. rival L1s; Walrus/DePIN traction; how the FTX-Ventures association ages; whether Sui accrues real value or stays narrative-beta.
persons.confidence: Moderate — the entity/funding/pedigree facts are documented; the leadership read is interpretation.
persons.grade: Solid on funding and pedigree facts (web-verified in the corpus); worldview/decision-style are reasoned inference.

persons.name: Sam Blackshear
persons.role: Creator of the Move language; Mysten Labs / Sui co-founder & CTO
persons.bluf: Blackshear designed the Move smart-contract language at Meta's Diem/Novi and co-founded Mysten Labs, where Move underpins Sui. He is primarily a programming-languages/technical figure rather than a public strategist; the corpus documents his creator role and Diem lineage. Assessment is technically grounded but leadership-thin.
persons.drivers: Designing a safer resource-oriented smart-contract language (Move) and seeing it adopted at scale; technical craft over public profile. Personal motivations beyond the technical are not detailed in the corpus.
persons.worldview: Programming-languages safety orientation (Move's linear/resource model aimed at asset-safety); a builder's rather than an ideologue's framing.
persons.decision_style: Not documented in the research files; his path (Diem research → co-founding a well-funded L1 to carry Move forward) implies a technology-first, build-the-substrate approach, but decision specifics are not evidenced.
persons.track_record: Created the Move language at Diem/Novi; co-founded Mysten Labs and shipped Sui, whose object model and Move VM are its technical differentiators. Part of the ex-Diem diaspora (Move also underpins Aptos).
persons.vulnerabilities: Same ecosystem exposures as Mysten/Sui (FTX-Ventures backing, debated token accrual, L1 competition); little independent legal/financial exposure documented.
persons.relationships: Co-founder/CEO Evan Cheng; the Move/Diem community (incl. Aptos, which also uses a Move variant); Mysten backers a16z and FTX Ventures.
persons.outlook: Watch: Move-language adoption across Sui/Aptos and beyond; Sui technical roadmap; whether Move becomes a durable standard.
persons.confidence: Low-to-Moderate — the Move-creator and co-founder facts are documented; strategic profile is thin.
persons.grade: Grounded on the Move-creator fact from the blockchain-ecosystem block; leadership/decision detail under-documented and kept brief.

persons.name: Keonne Rodriguez
persons.bluf: Rodriguez was CEO/co-founder of the non-custodial Bitcoin mixing wallet Samourai; charged by SDNY in April 2024 and, despite FinCEN's own 2023 view that the non-custodial software was not a money transmitter and an April 2025 DOJ memo telling prosecutors to stop targeting such tools, he pled guilty to unlicensed money-transmitting and was sentenced to 5 years (Nov 2025). His case is a central 'code-is-speech' / developer-liability data point in the corpus.
persons.drivers: Building financial-privacy tooling for Bitcoin (Whirlpool/Ricochet); a privacy-maximalist stance treating self-custodial mixing as a civil-liberty. The prosecution alleged the tool was used to launder >$100M of criminal proceeds — the counterweight held in view.
persons.worldview: Cypherpunk/financial-privacy: non-custodial, disintermediated code as protected speech and a right, positioned against the surveillance/identified-rails direction the corpus maps.
persons.decision_style: Risk-tolerant privacy advocacy under legal fire; ultimately took a guilty plea (Count II) after the money-laundering count was dismissed — a pragmatic resolution rather than an appellate test of the theory.
persons.track_record: Co-built and ran Samourai Wallet (Whirlpool coinjoin, Ricochet); operated it for years as a leading Bitcoin-privacy tool before the 2024 indictment and 2025 plea/sentence.
persons.vulnerabilities: Convicted (plea) and imprisoned (5 years); the disposition stands even amid the DOJ policy reversal and FinCEN's contrary guidance — the corpus's 'privacy devs stayed convicted while compliant firms won relief' asymmetry. No appellate vindication.
persons.relationships: Co-founder William Lonergan Hill ('TDev'); prosecutorial counterparts at SDNY/DOJ (Blanche memo context); parallel defendants Roman Storm (Tornado Cash) and Alexey Pertsev.
persons.outlook: Watch: any post-conviction relief or pardon in the deregulatory climate; how his case is cited in future developer-liability litigation; the chilling effect on US privacy tooling.
persons.confidence: High — the charges, FinCEN call, Brady dispute, plea, and sentence are documented and dated.
persons.grade: Strong on the legal record (DOJ/SDNY press, verdict analysis); the persecution-vs-legitimate-prosecution question is explicitly left open, not asserted.

persons.name: William Lonergan Hill
persons.role: Samourai Wallet co-founder / CTO ('TDev'); prosecuted (pled guilty)
persons.bluf: Hill, known as 'TDev', co-founded and served as CTO of Samourai Wallet; charged alongside Rodriguez in April 2024, he pled guilty to unlicensed money-transmitting and was sentenced to 4 years (Nov 2025). Like his co-founder, his conviction survived FinCEN's contrary 2023 guidance and the April 2025 DOJ memo — a core exhibit in the corpus's privacy-developer-liability thread.
persons.drivers: Building the technical core of a non-custodial Bitcoin privacy wallet; a privacy-engineering ethos treating disintermediated code as a right. The prosecution's laundering rationale (>$100M) is held alongside as the counterweight.
persons.worldview: Cypherpunk/financial-privacy, aligned with Rodriguez: self-custodial, censorship-resistant tooling as protected speech against identified, surveilled rails.
persons.decision_style: Long-running technical stewardship of a legally exposed tool; ultimately accepted a guilty plea after the laundering count was dismissed — resolution over an appellate fight.
persons.track_record: Co-built Samourai's non-custodial mixing technology (Whirlpool/Ricochet) as 'TDev'; operated it for years before the 2024 indictment and 2025 plea/4-year sentence.
persons.vulnerabilities: Convicted (plea) and imprisoned (4 years); no appellate ruling; the disposition persisted through the policy reversal — the 'small privacy devs lose while compliant incumbents win' asymmetry applies squarely to him.
persons.relationships: Co-founder Keonne Rodriguez; SDNY/DOJ prosecutors; parallel privacy-developer defendants Roman Storm and Alexey Pertsev.
persons.outlook: Watch: any post-conviction relief/pardon; citation of his case in developer-liability precedent; continued chilling of US-based privacy development.
persons.confidence: High — indictment, plea, and 4-year sentence are documented and dated.
persons.grade: Strong on the legal record; intent/bad-faith framing on either side is left as the open question per the corpus's neutral design.

persons.name: Zooko Wilcox
persons.bluf: Wilcox founded the Electric Coin Company and built Zcash, the zk-SNARK shielded-transaction coin the corpus frames as the cryptographically strongest — and notably non-prosecuted — financial-privacy contrast to Samourai/Tornado Cash. He is a veteran cypherpunk cryptographer who pursued privacy through peer-reviewed cryptography and a formal company rather than an anonymous protocol.
persons.drivers: Advancing strong, mathematically rigorous financial privacy (zero-knowledge proofs) as a public good; sustaining it through a legitimate, funded organization. A long-standing cypherpunk lineage (DigiCash-era, MojoNation, Tahoe-LAFS).
persons.worldview: Privacy-through-cryptography and open science: build auditable, peer-reviewed zk systems in the open rather than obfuscation — a stance that has so far coexisted with regulators better than mixer projects.
persons.decision_style: Institution- and research-forward: chose a company (ECC) with disclosed funding/dev structure and published cryptography, arguably lowering legal exposure relative to anonymous mixing tools.
persons.track_record: Co-created Zcash and its shielded (zk-SNARK) transaction model; built ECC as an ongoing steward; a durable figure in privacy-cryptography with pre-Zcash open-source pedigree.
persons.vulnerabilities: Privacy coins face exchange delistings and regulatory pressure as a category; Zcash's optional-shielding usage debates; dependence on ECC funding/governance. But no prosecution is documented — the point of the 'non-prosecuted contrast' framing.
persons.relationships: Electric Coin Company and the Zcash Foundation ecosystem; positioned in the corpus against prosecuted peers (Rodriguez/Hill, Storm) and alongside the broader privacy-asset frontier (Monero, Zano).
persons.outlook: Watch: regulatory treatment of privacy coins vs. mixers; Zcash adoption/shielded-usage trends; whether the 'strongest, non-prosecuted' status holds if the surveillance posture returns.
persons.confidence: Moderate — the founder/Zcash/zk-SNARK facts are documented; the leadership read draws on the well-established public record.
persons.grade: Solid on the documented founder role and technical framing; worldview is grounded interpretation, not asserted intent.

persons.name: Jeremy Kauffman
persons.role: Founder, LBRY (SEC case; company shut down)
persons.bluf: Kauffman founded LBRY, a decentralized content protocol that the SEC sued over its token sales; LBRY lost summary judgment (D.N.H., Nov 2022), dropped its appeal, and shut down in 2023, with its video front-end Odysee spun out to survive. The corpus frames LBRY as the clearest case of enforcement killing a builder rather than a fraud — and, notably, one never reversed even as big exchanges later saw dismissals.
persons.drivers: Building a decentralized, censorship-resistant content/publishing network; a free-speech and anti-platform-gatekeeper motivation carried into outspoken libertarian politics. Fighting the SEC action as a matter of principle.
persons.worldview: Libertarian / free-speech maximalist and anti-regulatory: publicly combative toward the SEC and the 'regulation by enforcement' posture, framing LBRY as a small builder crushed by agency overreach.
persons.decision_style: Confrontational and principle-driven: litigated the SEC rather than quietly settling, then wound the company down when it lost; spun out Odysee to preserve the product. High-risk, publicly vocal.
persons.track_record: Founded and ran LBRY and its protocol/token; built Odysee as the surviving frontend; lost the SEC case and shut LBRY down (2023). Became a prominent voice against crypto enforcement (SEC Commissioner Peirce dissented, calling the action 'overdue' to reconsider).
persons.vulnerabilities: LBRY was effectively bankrupted by the case; the loss was never reversed (unlike Coinbase/Ripple/Kraken) — the durable 'small firms die, large firms outlast the policy' asymmetry lands on him personally. Ongoing regulatory hostility to token-sale models.
persons.relationships: Odysee (spun-out successor); SEC (adversary; Peirce sympathetic dissent); the broader fair-notice/major-questions defender camp (e.g., John Deaton) the corpus maps.
persons.outlook: Watch: Odysee's survival; Kauffman's continued political/advocacy activity; whether the deregulatory turn produces any belated relief for LBRY-type casualties (unlikely — it died first).
persons.confidence: High — the D.N.H. ruling, dropped appeal, shutdown, and Odysee spinout are documented and dated.
persons.grade: Strong on the enforcement record; the 'builder not fraud' and asymmetry framing is labeled interpretation drawn from the corpus.

persons.name: Erik Voorhees
persons.role: ShapeShift founder; crypto-libertarian; founder of Venice.ai
persons.bluf: Voorhees is a veteran crypto-libertarian entrepreneur (ShapeShift) who now runs Venice.ai (token VVV on Base), a private/uncensored inference platform with no data storage or KYC — placing him in the corpus's decentralized-AI-access layer as a counter to hyperscaler and state control. He consistently builds tools that resist intermediation and identity-binding across both crypto and AI.
persons.drivers: Maximizing individual financial and informational sovereignty; monetizing censorship-resistant, no-KYC infrastructure (staking VVV for no-marginal-cost private inference). A long-running ideological commitment to permissionless systems.
persons.worldview: Explicit crypto-libertarian / anarcho-capitalist: privacy, no-KYC, censorship resistance, and disintermediation as first principles — applied first to money (ShapeShift) and now to AI inference (Venice).
persons.decision_style: Ideologically consistent and regulation-confronting: built no-KYC exchange tooling (ShapeShift's history with regulators) and now a no-data-storage AI service; willing to operate at the contested edge of compliance in service of the principle.
persons.track_record: Founded ShapeShift (early no-account crypto exchange) and other crypto ventures; a prominent public libertarian voice; launched Venice.ai on Base as a private/uncensored inference product (VVV token). Part of the corpus's local/decentralized-AI counter-ecosystem.
persons.vulnerabilities: Prior regulatory friction over ShapeShift's no-KYC model; Venice's no-KYC/uncensored positioning carries dual-use abuse-vector and potential regulatory exposure; crypto-AI tokens (incl. VVV) risk being narrative-beta whose value-accrual is contested.
persons.relationships: The decentralized-compute/access cohort in the corpus (Bittensor, Akash, Render, Octra) and sovereign-computing FUTO (Eron Wolf); broader crypto-libertarian community.
persons.outlook: Watch: Venice.ai/VVV traction and whether no-KYC private inference draws regulatory attention; how uncensored-AI dual-use is treated; his continued advocacy.
persons.confidence: Moderate — Venice.ai/VVV facts are web-verified in the corpus; ShapeShift and ideology rest on the well-established public record.
persons.grade: Solid on the Venice.ai facts from the local-uncensored-AI block plus established public record; ideology is well-documented interpretation.

persons.name: Eric Hartford
persons.role: Creator of the 'uncensored' Dolphin LLM fine-tunes
persons.bluf: Hartford created the Dolphin family of 'uncensored' fine-tunes — refusal-stripped versions of open models (Llama/Mistral/Mixtral/Qwen) — a core technique in the corpus's uncensored-AI layer alongside abliteration. His work is graded as fact and explicitly dual-use: it de-censors both Western safety-tuning and Chinese state-alignment, embodying the 'open-weight paradox' that total control is leaky by design.
persons.drivers: Removing alignment refusals to make open models fully steerable/uncensored; a stated view (per his writing) that models should follow the user rather than embed a provider's or state's guardrails. Advancing open-weight capability outside platform content controls.
persons.worldview: Open-weight freedom / user-sovereignty over model behavior: guardrails belong to the operator, not the vendor — a de-censorship stance that cuts against both corporate safety-tuning and state censorship.
persons.decision_style: Openly published, technique-forward: releases the method and the models publicly (fine-tuning on refusal-stripped data), accepting the dual-use trade-off rather than gatekeeping.
persons.track_record: Produced the widely used Dolphin fine-tunes across major open model families; a recognized figure in the uncensored/local-AI community. The technique is documented as fact and quality-variable in efficacy.
persons.vulnerabilities: Dual-use exposure — uncensored models are both a freedom tool and an abuse vector (the corpus grades this explicitly); reputational/regulatory attention as safety-removal draws scrutiny; efficacy/quality of de-censoring varies.
persons.relationships: The uncensored/local-AI ecosystem: abliteration tooling (Heretic/p-e-w), local runtimes (Ollama/llama.cpp), and the decentralized-inference cohort (Venice/Voorhees) that serves such models.
persons.outlook: Watch: how safety/regulatory pressure treats refusal-stripping; continued Dolphin releases against newer open models; whether abliteration (automated) supplants hand fine-tuning.
persons.confidence: Moderate — the Dolphin technique/authorship is documented as fact; motivations draw on his public writing.
persons.grade: Solid on the documented technique and dual-use grading from the local-uncensored-AI block; worldview is grounded in his public statements, labeled interpretation.

persons.name: Auren Hoffman
persons.role: Co-founder & chairman, Dialog; owner, Stonebrick LLC; CEO, SafeGraph/Flex Capital (data broker)
persons.bluf: The operator behind Dialog. Thiel is the namesake co-founder, but Hoffman actually runs the invitation-only society — through Stonebrick, LLC, a for-profit entity he owns — and built the A/B/C member-grading and AI-dossier machinery that leaked in June 2026. A career data-broker (SafeGraph/LiveRamp lineage). Assess with MODERATE-HIGH confidence on his operating role.
persons.drivers: Convening power; a durable elite trust-network; the data/relationship graph that Dialog's dossiers embody.
persons.worldview: Bipartisan-but-libertarian-leaning; treats access, matchmaking and candor as engineerable products.
persons.decision_style: Operator/product-builder; quantifies status (grades, value-add scores) and manages the invite list.
persons.track_record: Co-founded Dialog (2006) with Thiel; chaired the earlier Stonebrick Group networking events; founded data firms in the location-data/identity-resolution business.
persons.vulnerabilities: The June-2026 leak exposed member PII, political-leaning tags and matchmaking data held in an open Airtable — a discretion-as-identity society undone by his own operation's security failure.
persons.relationships: Co-founder with [[Peter_Thiel]]; employs ED [[Raffi_Grinberg]]; convenes the member-employer orbit (Palantir, OpenAI, Anduril, a16z, Founders Fund).
persons.outlook: Central operator of an elite-cohesion network now moving toward a permanent DC-area campus.
persons.confidence: MODERATE-HIGH (operating role documented; Stonebrick ownership single-outlet + public-records inference).

persons.role: Executive director, Dialog; author, 'How to Be a Grown-Up'
persons.bluf: The executive who runs Dialog's day-to-day operations — the invitation-only Thiel/Hoffman forum — keeping a deliberately low profile. His public footprint is minimal by design ('We intentionally keep a low profile; if you received an invite, you can reach me by email'). Assess with MODERATE confidence: role documented, internal authority beyond ops thinly sourced.

persons.worldview: Discretion-first operator of an off-the-record convening.

persons.track_record: Runs Dialog with a small team (incl. managing director Juliette Levine); wrote a book on early-adulthood skills.
persons.vulnerabilities: Presides over the operation whose member directory and dossiers leaked in June 2026; the secrecy doctrine proved aspirational.
persons.relationships: Reports into chairman [[Auren_Hoffman]]; namesake co-founder [[Peter_Thiel]] is at arm's length.
persons.outlook: The operational hand behind a society expanding toward a permanent institutional footprint.
